The terminology originated in signal detection theory (radar, sonar) and was adopted by medical testing — a positive result is one that detects the condition — it’s bad news for the patient, not good news. That distinction will matter when we talk about sensitivity and specificity.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: can we just run linear regression?
text:
can we just run linear regression?


predictions below 0 and above 1 — not valid probabilities

The obvious first attempt: fit OLS to the 0/1 labels. It runs. But the predictions are nonsense — many are negative, some exceed 1. Nothing constrains OLS to produce numbers in [0, 1]. We need a model that lives on the probability scale.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: the trick: squeeze the line through a sigmoid
text:
the trick: squeeze the line through a sigmoid


\[p = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_d x_d\]

Rather than constrain OLS, transform what we want to model. The linear predictor z = β₀ + β₁x₁ + ⋯ can be any real number. Push it through the sigmoid σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ) and the output is forced into (0, 1). The sigmoid is symmetric around zero: z = 0 maps to p = 0.5, big positive z maps toward 1, big negative z maps toward 0. Logistic regression is just linear regression on the log-odds of the outcome, then squashed through σ.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
sports bettor’s intuition
sportsbook: “the Celtics are 4-to-1 underdogs tonight”

Q: what probability of winning does that imply?


odds \(= \dfrac{P(\text{win})}{P(\text{lose})}\)
4-to-1 against → lose 4 games per 1 win → \(P(\text{win}) = \tfrac{1}{1+4} = 0.20\)


probability in \([0, 1]\) \(\leftrightarrow\) odds in \([0, \infty)\) — same info, different scale

Probe for prior knowledge. Most students have seen “3-to-1 odds” in sports, poker, or general English. Odds = P(win) / P(lose). 4-to-1 against means for every 1 win there are 4 losses, so P(win) = 1/5 = 0.20. The probability-odds correspondence is one-to-one — anything you can say about probabilities you can say about odds. The useful move is next: take the log and the range opens up further.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
from probabilities to log-odds



probability \(p\)
odds \(p/(1-p)\)
log-odds




0.01
0.01
\(-4.6\)


0.20
0.25
\(-1.4\)


0.50
1.00
\(\phantom{-}0.0\)


0.80
4.00
\(+1.4\)


0.99
99.0
\(+4.6\)




log-odds range from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\) — perfect for a linear model
\[\log\frac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots\]

Same math, different way to see it. Odds = p / (1-p), familiar from sports betting. Odds range from 0 to infinity. Take the log and the range opens up to the full real line. That’s what we model with a linear function of x. The log-odds are called the logit, and logistic regression is the claim that the logit is linear in the features.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
check your intuition
Pr(Win) = 0.6 → odds = \(\frac{0.6}{0.4} = 1.5\)

I double your odds → new odds = 3.0


Q: new Pr(Win) = ?


\[\text{Pr(Win)} = \frac{3.0}{1 + 3.0} = 0.75\]
doubling odds \(\neq\) doubling probability — the scales are nonlinear

INLINE Q/A: Think-pair-share (3 min) Prompt: Pr(Win) = 0.6. I double the odds. What’s the new probability? Process goal: compute on a nonlinear scale Common wrong answer: “1.20” (doubled 0.6) — breaks immediately since probabilities can’t exceed 1. Subtler wrong answer: “0.80” from vague doubling logic. The correct answer (0.75) requires converting to odds, doubling, converting back. If stuck: “Convert to odds first — what are the odds when Pr(Win) = 0.6?” Key insight: This is why logistic regression coefficients are hard to interpret on the probability scale: a one-unit increase in a feature multiplies the odds by \(e^\beta\), but the effect on probability depends on where you start.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
logistic regression on age alone


predicted risk climbs with age — we see the lower portion of the S-curve because the base rate is low

Fit logistic regression with just age as the feature so we can plot the whole decision curve. The curve rises from under 5% for the youngest patients to over 30% for the oldest. We’re looking at the lower tail of the S — the full sigmoid would sweep through 0.5 and level off at 1, but the base rate of CHD is low enough that age alone doesn’t get anyone to 50% predicted risk. When we add more features, each patient’s predicted probability depends on nine risk factors, not just age — and no one feature tells the whole story.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: where does the loss come from?
text:
where does the loss come from?
MLE (maximum likelihood estimation): pick \(\beta\) to makes observed labels most probable

for one observation with label \(y \in \{0, 1\}\) and predicted probability \(p\):
\[P(y \mid x) = p^{\,y} \, (1-p)^{1-y}\]


likelihood of \(n\) observations. independent, so multiply:
\[L(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^n p_i^{\,y_i} \, (1-p_i)^{1-y_i}\]


take \(-\log\): products → sums, maximize → minimize
\[-\log L(\beta) = -\sum_{i=1}^n \big[ y_i \log p_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-p_i) \big]\]

Every loss function comes from somewhere — usually a modeling assumption. For logistic regression, the loss drops out of maximum likelihood. Treat each label as a Bernoulli draw with parameter p = σ(xβ). The per-observation probability is p^y (1-p)^(1-y): evaluates to p if y=1, to (1-p) if y=0. Multiply across independent observations to get the likelihood. Take the log to turn products into sums, then negate — maximizing likelihood becomes minimizing negative log-likelihood, which is the log loss. So the loss isn’t pulled out of a hat — it’s the direct consequence of assuming Bernoulli labels.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the logistic loss
\[\ell(\beta) = -\big[ y \log(p) + (1-y) \log(1-p) \big]\]
also called cross-entropy or negative log-likelihood

penalizes confident wrong predictions especially hard

\(y = 1\), \(p \to 0\): \(-\log(p) \to \infty\)
\(y = 0\), \(p \to 1\): \(-\log(1-p) \to \infty\)



Q: if \(y = 1\) and \(p = 0.9\), loss = \(-\log(0.9)\) ≈ ? what about \(p = 0.1\)?



\(-\log(0.9) = 0.11\) (confident, correct → small)
\(-\log(0.1) = 2.3\) (confident, wrong → large)



no closed-form minimum. we’ll need an iterative solver.

Quick computation to anchor the formula before the visual comparison. The single-observation form of the loss. If the label is 1 and we predicted p, the loss is -log(p); if the label is 0, it’s -log(1-p). Either way, the loss is small when we’re right with high confidence and large when we’re wrong with high confidence. “Confident wrong” is punished severely — that’s why logistic regression picks coefficients that aren’t overconfident on hard cases. Unlike linear regression, there’s no closed-form minimum — the sigmoid makes the gradient nonlinear in β. That motivates gradient descent on the next slide.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
squared error vs. logistic loss


a 10% prediction for a true 1? squared error 0.81, logistic loss 2.3


the loss function determines what the model finds

The comparison visually. When the prediction is near 1 (correct, confident), both losses are small. As the prediction drops toward 0 for a true 1 — a confident wrong answer — squared error stays below 1, but logistic loss explodes. That asymmetry is what forces logistic regression to avoid overconfidence on hard cases. The one-liner: the loss function determines what the model finds. Choose squared error and the model minimizes average distance; choose logistic loss and the model minimizes confident-wrong predictions. The loss is a design choice, not a given.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
gradient descent: hiking downhill


\[\beta \leftarrow \beta - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\beta)\]

\(\nabla L(\beta)\) = gradient (uphill direction); step the opposite way
\(\eta\) = learning rate (step size)

logistic loss is convex — one valley, no false minima




Picture the loss as a landscape over the β coefficients. Gradient descent is literal hiking. At each step, compute the gradient — the vector of partial derivatives, which points uphill — and step the opposite direction. η is the step size. Too small and it’s slow; too big and you overshoot. The magic property of logistic regression is that the landscape is convex: a single bowl with no false valleys. Gradient descent finds the global minimum regardless of starting point. Callback: in Chapter 5, linear regression had the normal equations — a closed-form solution. The sigmoid makes the loss nonlinear in β, so there’s no formula. We iterate instead. Edge case worth mentioning if a student asks: if the data is perfectly separable (a line can perfectly separate the two classes), the sigmoid can always be pushed closer to 0 or 1, so coefficients grow without bound and no finite minimum exists. The loss is still convex — it’s the minimum that runs off to infinity.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
watch it converge
Q: will the loss curve drop smoothly, oscillate, or bounce around?

commit to a prediction — then we run 50 iterations

INLINE Q/A: Predict-then-reveal (1 min) Prompt: Will the loss curve drop smoothly, oscillate, or bounce around? Process goal: predict convergence behavior from convexity Common wrong answer: “oscillate” — reasonable, since gradient descent can oscillate with too-large a step size. But with η = 0.5 on a convex problem, we’ll see smooth descent. If stuck: “We said the loss is convex — one valley. Does that help?” Key insight: The commitment matters more than the answer — students who predicted the wrong shape will remember the reveal.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
watch it converge


50 iterations on age + BMI (body mass index) logistic regression. loss drops fast, then settles.

Reveal. The loss drops sharply in the first handful of iterations, then settles. Because the problem is convex, “settles” means we’ve found the minimum. sklearn’s LogisticRegression uses a fancier variant (L-BFGS or similar), but the intuition is the same: start somewhere, step downhill, stop when the gradient is small.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: accuracy lies under class imbalance

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
fit it, test it
# setup: Framingham data, 9 features, standardized, 70/30 split
model = LogisticRegression(penalty=None, max_iter=1000).fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
test_acc = model.score(X_test_scaled, y_test)
print(f"Test accuracy: {test_acc:.2f}")

Test accuracy: 0.855


85.5% accurate (at default threshold 0.5) — sounds great, right?

Fit our 9-feature logistic regression on the Framingham training set — the full feature set, with standardized features. Compute accuracy on the held-out test set. 85.5%. A B+ on a typical grading curve. Hold this number in mind. Now: what’s the accuracy of the dumbest possible model?

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
but wait
the “always predict no CHD” baseline:
Q: what accuracy does it get?

baseline accuracy  = 0.848
our model accuracy = 0.855
improvement        = 0.007


our fancy classifier barely beats the null







accuracy measures how often you’re right overall — when one class dominates, that’s easy and uninformative

Predict “no CHD” for every single patient — no features, no fitting, nothing. Its accuracy is 1 - base rate = 85% because 85% of patients actually don’t develop CHD. Our model’s 85.5% is only slightly better. The 85.5% accuracy number was almost entirely driven by class imbalance, not by the model learning anything useful. This is the accuracy trap — and it shows up whenever one class dominates. Accuracy is a population-averaged metric, weighted by how often each class appears. In an imbalanced problem (medical screening, fraud, equipment failure, spam), the common class dominates the average. Metrics that weight the two classes differently — or that look at each class separately — tell a more honest story. We need different metrics.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the confusion matrix
1,096 test patients · 15% CHD base rate · threshold 0.5









predicted no CHD
predicted CHD




actually no CHD
TN (true negative) = ?
FP (false positive) = ?


actually CHD
FN (false negative) = ?
TP (true positive) = ?




predict: of 167 actual CHD cases, how many does the model catch?

fewer than 20
20–50
50–100
more than 100


INLINE Q/A: Poll (1 min) Prompt: Of 167 actual CHD cases, how many does the model catch? Process goal: predict a magnitude Common wrong answer: “50–100” or “more than 100” — students anchor on the 85% accuracy and assume the model catches most cases. The real answer (17) is shocking. Most students will pick too high, which sets up the precision/recall reveal perfectly. If stuck: “The model is very conservative — it predicts ‘no CHD’ for almost everyone. What does that mean for the bottom row?” Key insight: The TN cell dominates. The model gets 85% accuracy by correctly predicting the easy cases and missing almost all the hard ones.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
the confusion matrix


of 167 actual CHD cases, we caught 17

Reveal. True negatives dominate (922) — exactly as predicted. But the bottom row is the story: 17 caught, 150 missed. The model is correctly saying “not CHD” to most people and missing the ones that matter. Accuracy hid this because the 922 true negatives drown out everything else.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
precision vs. recall


precision \[\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}\]
“I flagged this patient — should I trust the flag?”

recall (sensitivity) \[\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}\]
“of all the sick people, how many did we find?”


F1 score = harmonic mean of precision and recall — one number, but hides the cost asymmetry


two metrics → two different stories

Precision is the precision of your positive predictions — when you say yes, how often are you right? Recall is the completeness of your positive detection — of all the true positives out there, how many did you catch? They move in opposite directions when you change the threshold. Require higher confidence before flagging → precision up, recall down. Lower the bar → more flags, precision drops, recall climbs.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
our model’s precision and recall
Precision: 0.65
  → Of patients flagged as CHD, 65% actually had it

Recall:    0.10
  → Of actual CHD patients (167 in test set), we caught 17

accuracy hid a disaster — the model catches almost nobody

Precision is decent — when the model does flag someone, about two-thirds of the time it’s right. But recall is abysmal. Of all 167 actual CHD patients in the test set, we caught 17. For a screening tool this is a complete failure. The 85% accuracy masked that the model is barely functioning on the task we actually care about.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
which type of error would you rather the model make?

CHD screening:

FP → $500 follow-up
FN → $50K emergency care

spam filter:

FP → real email lost → missed meeting
FN → spam in inbox → mild annoyance


for each: flag more, or flag fewer?


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min — 30 sec think, 1 min neighbor). Prompt: Which type of error would you rather the model make — for CHD screening and for spam? Process goal: cost-benefit reasoning about asymmetric errors Common wrong answer: students who focus on the medical case may say “FP is also bad because of patient anxiety” — valid, but the dollar asymmetry (500x) makes FN clearly worse. The interesting split comes from students who reason differently about spam vs. medicine. If stuck: “Start with medicine. A missed heart attack costs $50K. A follow-up test costs $500. Which mistake is cheaper?” Key insight: The right threshold depends on the business problem, not the statistics. Threshold choice is a cost-benefit call. Callback: “positive” = condition detected, not condition desired. A false positive means we flagged someone who is actually healthy.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: when is a flag worth it?
text:
when is a flag worth it?
flag if the expected savings beat the expected cost

flag whenever P(disease | flagged) > break-even precision
\[p^* = \frac{1}{k+1}\]
where \(k = C_{FN}/C_{FP}\) is the cost ratio of a missed case to a false alarm


CHD: \(k = 100 \Rightarrow p^* \approx 1\%\) — push hard for recall
spam: \(k \ll 1 \Rightarrow p^* \approx 99\%\) — protect precision

Make threshold choice quantitative. For a single patient with predicted probability \(p\): flagging costs \((1-p)C_{FP}\) in expectation; not flagging costs \(p \cdot C_{FN}\). Flag when the first is smaller, which gives \(p > 1/(k+1)\) where \(k\) is the cost ratio. Call that threshold the “break-even precision” \(p^*\).
Why “precision” and not just “probability”? Because on the PR curve, precision IS the conditional probability \(P(\text{disease} \mid \text{flagged})\) — that’s the definition. So the rule reads off directly from the y-axis of the PR curve.
Two extreme calibrations: CHD has \(k=100\) ($50K vs $500), so \(p^* \approx 1\%\) — almost any flag is worth it. Spam has \(k\) much less than 1 (lost real email > extra spam), so \(p^* \approx 99\%\) — flag only when nearly certain.
If a sharp student asks “isn’t precision the average over flagged patients, not the marginal next flag?” — yes. The rule is slightly conservative because marginal precision sits below average precision. That’s fine — the cost ratio itself is an estimate, so a conservative margin is welcome.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
precision-recall across thresholds


each annotated point is one threshold — as recall climbs, precision falls


precision is P(disease | flagged) — read the y-axis as trustworthiness of a flag


stop where precision still beats \(p^*\) — beyond that, the marginal flag loses money

Rather than pick a single threshold, sweep all of them. For each threshold, compute precision and recall and plot the point. Perfect model: a point at (1, 1). At t = 0.5 (top-left), precision is decent but recall is terrible. Slide down-and-right and you catch more real cases but each flag is less trustworthy.
Now apply the break-even rule from the previous slide. The y-axis literally is \(P(\text{disease} \mid \text{flagged})\) — that’s the definition of precision. So the rule “flag when \(P > p^*\)” translates directly to “stay above the horizontal line \(y = p^*\) on the PR curve.” Slide down the curve until precision approaches \(p^*\) and stop.
For our CHD model with \(k=100\), \(p^* \approx 1\%\) — every annotated point on this curve sits well above break-even, so the rule says push the threshold even lower than what’s plotted.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
another view: ROC (receiver operating characteristic)


AUC (area under the curve) ≈ 0.75 — concordance: pick one CHD and one non-CHD patient; the model ranks the CHD patient higher 75% of the time (ties count as half)

Same threshold sweep, different axes: plot true positive rate (= recall) against false positive rate (= FP / all negatives). Perfect model: hugs the top-left corner. Random guessing: the diagonal. AUC = area under. Our 0.75 has a clean interpretation — concordance probability. Pick one CHD patient and one non-CHD patient; the model ranks the CHD patient higher 75% of the time. That’s threshold-free ranking quality. Let the 0.75 sit — the subgroup and calibration sections that follow will show why a decent aggregate AUC still hides trouble.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: PR vs ROC — when to use which
text:
PR vs ROC — when to use which


PR curve
axes: precision · recall
depends on base rate
operational view — most honest when the positive class is rare

ROC curve
axes: TPR (true positive rate = recall) · FPR (false positive rate)
invariant to base rate
threshold-free summary (AUC) · compare models across datasets


same tradeoff, different invariances

Two cuts of the same threshold sweep. The PR curve speaks to the operational question — “when I flag a patient, am I right?” — which is exactly what matters in imbalanced screening. It depends on the base rate, so it’s not directly comparable across populations. The ROC curve and its AUC are invariant to how many negatives you have: shuffle in ten times more healthy patients from the same population and the ROC doesn’t change, while PR gets worse. (Caveat: if you move to a genuinely different population, the score distributions may shift and the ROC can change.) So for a fixed problem with a rare positive class — CHD, fraud, readmissions — the PR curve is usually the more honest view. For cross-dataset comparison or a ranking-quality summary, use ROC + AUC. In this class we’ll typically report both.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
you’re reporting to the hospital board on your CHD model
do you show the PR curve or the ROC curve?
one-sentence justification


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min — 30 sec commit to a curve, 1 min defend to a neighbor). Prompt: PR curve or ROC curve for the hospital board report? Process goal: apply the PR-vs-ROC distinction to a specific stakeholder Common wrong answer: “ROC because AUC is the standard metric.” Reasonable — but the board cares about operational performance on a rare condition (15% positive rate), and PR is the more honest view when the positive class is rare. ROC can look decent (AUC 0.75) while PR reveals the precision-recall tradeoff that drives staffing and follow-up costs. If stuck: “What question does each curve answer? Which question does the board care about?” Key insight: PR for operational decisions on rare conditions; ROC for comparing models across datasets. The audience determines the chart.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
threshold effect on metrics


low threshold → catches more, but each flag is less reliable

Concrete numbers. At the default 0.5 threshold, accuracy is 86%, precision 65%, recall 10% — we flag only 26 of 1,096 test patients. Drop the threshold to 0.1 and we flag 624 patients (57% of the test set). Recall jumps to 85% — we catch almost all CHD cases — but precision drops to 23% (most flags are wrong) and accuracy falls to 54%, below baseline. What to pick? Depends on follow-up test cost, ICU bed availability, patient outcomes. No purely statistical answer.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text: aggregate metrics hide local failures

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
a vendor pitches a cancer screening test as 99% accurate:

99% sensitivity — catches 99% of cancer cases
99% specificity — correctly clears 99% of healthy patients

cancer prevalence: 0.5%
a patient tests positive — P(cancer | positive)?
A. 99% · B. 67% · C. 33% · D. 1%


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min — commit by hands A/B/C/D, then work it out on 10,000 patients with a neighbor). Prompt: P(cancer | positive) given 99% sensitivity, 99% specificity, 0.5% prevalence? Process goal: Bayesian reasoning under base rate neglect Common wrong answer: A (99%) — most people anchor on the test accuracy and ignore the base rate. Even most doctors in published studies get this wrong (Casscells, Schoenberger & Graboys, 1978, NEJM). The correct answer is C (~33%). If stuck: “Start with 10,000 patients. How many actually have cancer at 0.5% prevalence? How many of those does the test catch? How many healthy patients does it falsely flag?” Key insight: False positives swamp true positives when the condition is rare. A “99% accurate” test yields only 33% PPV at 0.5% prevalence. Note: we’re being generous to the vendor by reading “99% accurate” as 99% sensitivity AND specificity. In practice, vendor claims often mean overall accuracy — achievable by predicting the majority class for everyone (the accuracy trap from earlier).

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
work it out on 10,000 patients


false positives swamp true positives when the condition is rare

Out of 10,000 patients: 50 actually have cancer (0.5% prevalence). Of those, the test catches 99%, so ~50 true positives. The other 9,950 are healthy. The test misclassifies 1% of them, so ~100 false positives. Total positive results: ~150. Of those 150, only ~50 are real — about 33%. The vendor’s “99% accuracy” number was true; the patient’s posterior probability was just 33%. That’s base rate neglect, and it’s why headline accuracy numbers on rare conditions are mostly meaningless.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
aggregate AUC is 0.75 · recall: age is the model’s strongest signal
predict: which age group does the model fail on?

under 40
40–54
55+

commit to a group — and say why


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min — 30 sec commit to a group, 1 min defend to a neighbor). Prompt: Which age group does the model fail on, and why? Process goal: predict + explain mechanism Common wrong answer: “55+ because older patients are more complex / heterogeneous.” Reasonable — but wrong. The model does best on older patients because age is its dominant signal. Under-40 patients have AUC = 0.36 because the positive rate is tiny and age-as-signal carries no information. If stuck: “Think about where the model’s strongest signal — age — stops helping.” Key insight: Aggregate AUC hides subgroup failure. The model learned “older = riskier” and has nothing useful for young patients — exactly the group where early detection matters most.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
subgroup AUC by age


for patients under 40, AUC = 0.36 — below 0.5, but based on only ~6 CHD cases; the estimate is noisy

Compute AUC separately in each age group. For patients over 40, the model is decent — AUC above 0.6 across groups. For under-40 patients, AUC drops to 0.36 — below 0.5, worse than random guessing. Inverting the model’s ranking would actually do better in this subgroup. The positive class there is tiny — only ~6 CHD cases in 191 patients — so the estimate is noisy, but the message is unambiguous: the model has no useful signal for young patients. It learned “older people get heart disease” and nothing else. Side note worth making if time permits: the Framingham cohort is drawn from a historically homogeneous population (mostly white, one town in Massachusetts). The subgroup failure we see by age is just the beginning — a responsible deployment would also check performance by race, sex, and insurance type.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
section: calibration — do probabilities mean what they say?
text:
calibration — do probabilities mean what they say?








bin predictions into deciles
does “10% risk” mean 10% observed rate?
. . .
AUC measures ranking
calibration measures absolute probabilities
. . .
a model can have good AUC and bad calibration (or vice versa) — which matters depends on how you use the output


The last aggregate lie to unmask. Logistic regression outputs probabilities, not just classifications. But do those probabilities mean what they claim? If the model says “20% risk” for a group, do 20% of that group actually develop CHD? To check: bin predictions into deciles (10 equal-count buckets), compute the observed CHD rate in each bin, plot observed vs predicted. A perfectly calibrated model sits on the diagonal. Our model tracks the diagonal reasonably well across the range where most patients live. It’s well-calibrated — even though we just saw it fails badly on other dimensions. Calibration is orthogonal to ranking: AUC measures only the order of predictions (rescale and AUC is unchanged); calibration depends on the actual values. A model that outputs “0.20” for patients truly at 0.40 risk has perfect ranking but awful calibration. If you use probabilities directly for cost-benefit tradeoffs, calibration matters. If you just pick top-k patients to screen, only ranking matters.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
back to the readmissions hook
the question that opened the lecture
a hospital’s model is “85% accurate” — deploy it?

now you know what to ask:

what’s the baseline? (accuracy trap)
what’s precision and recall at the chosen threshold?
ROC curve — is the chosen threshold a good point on the tradeoff?
does the model work across all patient groups?
are the probabilities calibrated?


Close the loop. We opened with a hospital’s 85% accurate readmissions model. Now you have the toolkit to diagnose it. First, check the baseline — is “predict none” 85%? If so, accuracy alone tells you nothing. Next, look at precision and recall at whatever threshold is being used. Then the full ROC curve and AUC to see performance across thresholds. Then subgroup breakdowns — is the model equally good across age, race, insurance type? Finally, calibration — if the model says “40% readmission risk,” is it really 40%? The hospital’s decision on where to operate on the precision-recall curve is a cost-benefit call about intervention dollars, staff availability, and patient outcomes — not a statistics question.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
key takeaways

accuracy is misleading with imbalanced classes — check the baseline first
the loss function determines what the model finds: logistic loss penalizes confident wrong answers
gradient descent on a convex loss finds the global minimum (unique when the data isn’t separable)
precision / recall reveal what accuracy hides — the right threshold is a cost-benefit call
AUC summarizes ranking across thresholds; calibration checks absolute probabilities
always check subgroup performance — aggregate metrics hide local failures


Six takeaways. The first three are about building a classifier: loss design, optimization, fitting. The last three are about evaluating one. The thread through all six: don’t trust a single number. Every aggregate can lie, and the way you dig into the lie depends on how your downstream decision uses the model.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
next time

Chapter 8: bootstrap — quantify uncertainty in any estimate (accuracy, AUC, odds ratio, …)
Chapter 12: formal inference on logistic coefficients (confidence intervals, p-values)
Chapter 13: decision trees — same classification problem, very different model


Today closed Act 1. Act 2 opens with the bootstrap — a resampling tool that lets us put confidence intervals on any statistic, including today’s classification metrics. Then Chapter 12 returns to logistic regression to do formal inference on coefficients. And in Act 3, trees and forests extend classification to nonlinear decision boundaries.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Logistic Regression and Metrics
text:
one-minute feedback



what was the most useful thing you learned today?
what was the most confusing?

give feedback




Closing ritual (1 min). Students fill out the form before leaving — silent, individual, not a discussion. The feedback is read and drives the next round of edits. Keep the slide up until the room clears.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
logistics

HW 4 out today — Airbnb PCA + clustering + Kaggle challenge
Quiz 7 Wednesday May 20 — PCA + clustering
project midterm returned this week


START RECORDING.
HW 4 covers Ch 12–15 (regression inference + diagnostics, trees, PCA + clustering). Part B is a Kaggle-style prediction challenge — best test-set score using anything you like. Quiz 7 covers the unsupervised pair (PCA Wednesday last week + clustering today).

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
the brief


Airbnb host, NYC
goal: figure out who you compete with so you can price against them
a cozy Brooklyn studio doesn’t compete with a Manhattan penthouse
ask the data: what is my competitive set?








The decision frame: a host wants a competitive set so they can price against comparable listings. Last lecture’s PCA gave us directions; today we want groups.
The image is visual furniture — students will see it again in block 1 as the (disappointing) first-run output. Don’t analyze it on this slide. Don’t preview the comparison in block 3.
The chapter’s punchline: clustering will dutifully produce three groups. Which three depends entirely on which features we hand it. If we include latitude and longitude, the clusters basically become boroughs — useless, because the host already knows the borough column. Drop lat/lon and the clusters become market segments (budget vs. moderate vs. premium) — actionable.
About 1 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
today

k-means — assign, recompute, repeat
choosing k, checking stability — what the standard tools tell you (and what they don’t)
your features pick your clusters — and that’s the whole game


Three beats. Block 1: the algorithm + a first run. Block 2: the validation tools (elbow, silhouette, seed stability) — none of them oracle the answer. Block 3: the chapter’s reveal — drop lat/lon, get genuinely useful segments. Plus real-world cases where the same mistake mattered.
About 30 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
bridge from PCA









PCA
k-means




structure found
directions of variance
groups of rows


representation
continuous (low-dim coords)
discrete (one label per row)


standardize first
yes — distances depend on units
yes — same reason


labels needed
no
no



both are unsupervised. PCA compresses; k-means clusters.

The bridge slide. PCA (last lecture) found low-dimensional directions — every row got a continuous PC1, PC2 score. K-means today finds groups — every row gets one discrete cluster label.
Both are unsupervised — no y column, no “right answer” to predict. Both rely on Euclidean distance, so both need standardization for the same reason.
There’s also a deeper connection (mathematically, both approximate the data matrix in low rank — PCA with continuous coordinates, k-means with one-hot indicators) but that’s a connection for a more advanced class, not for slide 5.
About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text: assign, recompute, repeat

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
the data
five Airbnb listings — what we hand the algorithm



price
bedrooms
accommodates
reviews
room type




75
1
2
142
private room


220
2
4
38
entire home


65
0
1
305
shared room


410
3
6
11
entire home


95
1
2
87
private room




no y column. no “right answer”. the algorithm finds structure on its own.

Toy table — five listings. The point: an unsupervised problem looks identical to a supervised one except there’s no y column. We’re not predicting anything; we’re asking the data to reveal structure.
room_type is categorical — we’ll need to one-hot encode it before running k-means (the algorithm uses Euclidean distance, and “private room” being 1 vs. “entire home” being 2 has no meaningful distance).
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
k-means in four steps


k-means clustering

partition n points into k groups by repeating:

assign each point to the nearest centroid
recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points

until assignments stop changing



centroid — the mean of all points in a cluster
starts from k random centroids
converges in finite steps (assignments are discrete; objective only goes down)


The algorithm in a definition box. Two operations: assign points to centroids, recompute centroids from their assigned points. Iterate.
The two glosses: - centroid = cluster mean (one per cluster) - finite convergence — there are finitely many ways to assign n points to k clusters, and each step strictly decreases (or holds) the objective. So we can’t cycle forever.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
watch it iterate


The 2D viz from the chapter — clustering Airbnb on just price and number of reviews (standardized) so we can plot it. k=3. Three colors are three clusters; the X markers are the centroids.
Panel 1: random initial centroids. Panel 2-3: centroids move toward the centers of their assigned points. Panel 4: stable — assignments have basically stopped changing.
The motion is the algorithm. Each panel: every point picks its nearest X; then each X moves to the average of its new neighborhood. Repeat.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
the objective


inertia (within-cluster sum of squares)

\text{inertia} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2
total squared distance from each point to its assigned centroid



each iteration strictly decreases inertia (or holds it)
objective is non-convex — different starts can land at different local minima


The formal objective. K-means minimizes the sum of squared distances from each point to its assigned centroid. C_k is the set of indices assigned to cluster k; \mu_k is that cluster’s centroid.
Two key properties: 1. Each step monotonically decreases inertia — that’s how we know it converges. The assignment step picks the nearest centroid (can’t increase any term); the recompute step uses the mean (which minimizes squared distance from a set of points). Both reduce inertia.

Non-convex. Different random initial centroids can converge to different local minima. We’ll come back to this in block 2 — the algorithm is not deterministic across random seeds.

About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
one more thing first — standardize
k-means uses Euclidean distance
→ a feature in dollars (range 10–10,000) dominates a feature in degrees latitude (range 40.5–40.9)




feature
unstandardized range
influence on distance




price
10–10,000
enormous


latitude
40.5–40.9
invisible





same lesson as PCA: standardize before any distance-based method

Standardization is essential for k-means — exactly the same reason as PCA (last lecture). Both use Euclidean distance; both let the largest-magnitude feature dominate if you don’t standardize.
The Airbnb concrete: price spans four orders of magnitude; latitude spans 0.4 degrees. Without standardization, all the “distance” between two listings is just their price difference. Latitude contributes essentially nothing.
If anyone asks why we don’t just scale price down — that’s exactly what standardization does, just symmetrically across all features. StandardScaler subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation of each column.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
run it — all the features
features = ['price', 'latitude', 'longitude', 'accommodates',
            'bedrooms', 'number_of_reviews', 'availability_365',
            'minimum_nights'] + room_dummies

X = StandardScaler().fit_transform(listings[features])

kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42, n_init=10)
listings['cluster'] = kmeans.fit_predict(X)

Cluster sizes:
0    13,442
1       651
2    15,049

The actual fit. Eight numerical features + four one-hot columns for room_type. Standardize, fit, predict.
Three clusters of very unequal size: ~16k, ~12k, and a tiny ~700. That tiny cluster is interesting — could be a niche segment (large groups? entire-floor rentals?) or could be a numerical artifact.
random_state=42 makes this reproducible; n_init=10 runs the algorithm 10 times from different random starts and keeps the best (lowest inertia). More on n_init in block 2.
About 75 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
look at the clusters


they trace geography. is that useful?

The map. Clusters plotted by longitude, latitude. The dominant cluster (green) covers Manhattan, Brooklyn, much of Queens. The other two carve out Queens/Bronx and a scattered third.
The visual punchline: the clusters mostly follow geography. They look like (a slightly garbled version of) boroughs.
Is that useful? Not really. We already have a neighbourhood_group_cleansed column with five boroughs. K-means just spent its effort rediscovering it — and badly.
Hold this for block 3 — we’ll fix it there. For now: the algorithm ran, produced clusters, and the result is anticlimactic.
About 1 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
before we move on — predict.
we got clusters. what’s the first question you should ask before reporting them to your host?
pick one.

is k=3 the right number?
are the clusters stable across reruns?
what do the clusters actually represent?
would different features give different clusters?



DISCUSSION: poll (2 min). Quick hands. No debrief beyond a sentence — this sets up the rest of the lecture.
All four are correct questions. The lecture answers them in order: - k=3? → block 2 (elbow + silhouette) - stable? → block 2 (random seeds + ARI) - what do they represent? → block 3 (interpret profiles) - different features? → block 3 (the reveal)
The point of the poll is to make students commit to a concern before we tour them. Whatever they pick is fine. If you want a one-line debrief: “all four are necessary — clustering looks easy because it runs. validating it is the hard part.”
About 2 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text: choose k. check stability. neither will save you.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
elbow + silhouette: the standard tools


elbow method

plot inertia vs. k; look for a bend where adding clusters stops helping much



silhouette score

for each point: how similar to its own cluster vs. the nearest other cluster
range [-1, 1]: near +1 = sits comfortably in its cluster, 0 = on a boundary, -1 = probably misassigned


Two definitions stacked. Elbow: inertia always decreases as k grows (more clusters → smaller within-cluster distances), so we don’t pick on inertia directly — we pick on where it stops decreasing fast. Silhouette: a per-point quality score in [-1, 1], averaged across all points; the higher, the more cleanly separated.
These are the two heuristics every clustering tutorial reaches for. Watch what they say on real data.
About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
elbow plot — no bend



no clear bend — inertia decreases steadily, typical of real data
silhouette peaks at k=2: just split the data in half
both are inconclusive


The two diagnostic plots from the chapter — elbow on the left, silhouette on the right.
Left (elbow): inertia drops smoothly from ~245k at k=2 down to ~120k at k=10. No bend. The annotation reads “no clear elbow — typical of real data.” This is what students will see on most real clustering problems: the curve is monotone and smooth, and the choice of k is arbitrary.
Right (silhouette): peaks at k=2 (~0.30), drops as k grows. The highest silhouette comes from the most trivial cluster structure: split into two halves.
Takeaway: on this dataset, the two standard heuristics give different answers, and neither is decisive. Choosing k is a judgment call, not a formula.
If asked: these heuristics aren’t useless — they can rule out clearly bad choices (e.g., silhouette score crashing toward 0 at k=15 tells you not to use 15). But they don’t pick k for you.
About 2 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
predict: same data, same k, different runs.
we run k-means 10 times with k=3, each time from a different random initialization. (no other change.)
how often do the 10 runs produce the same clusters?


always — the algorithm is deterministic


usually — small differences, basically the same


sometimes — depends on the data


rarely — the algorithm jumps to different solutions




DISCUSSION: poll (2 min). Set up the seed-instability reveal.
Most students think clustering is deterministic — “same data, same algorithm, same answer”. Wrong. K-means starts from random centroids, the objective is non-convex, and different starts can converge to different local minima.
Target: (c) — sometimes. Most pairs agree, but a meaningful fraction disagree by a lot. Reveal on the next slide.
If anyone picks (a): “let’s check.”

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
different seeds, different clusters


adjusted Rand index (ARI)

agreement between two clusterings, adjusted for chance

\text{ARI} = 1 → identical clusters
\text{ARI} = 0 → random agreement



so we can compare two runs of k-means with one number

The ARI is how we’ll quantify agreement between two clusterings. For any pair of points, ask: do both runs put them together, or split them apart?
ARI = 1 means the two runs partition every pair the same way (identical clusters, up to relabeling). ARI = 0 means the two runs agree no more than chance.
Compared to plain Rand index: ARI subtracts off the agreement you’d expect from randomly relabeling, so it’s a fair score even when one clustering is much more unbalanced than the other.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
10 runs, pairwise ARI



most pairs agree perfectly (ARI = 1.00)
runs 3 and 7 are different clusterings (ARI ≈ 0.72, 0.76)
same data, same algorithm, same k → not the same clusters


The heatmap from the chapter. Each cell is the ARI between two of the 10 runs (with n_init=1, so each is one random start).
Most cells are dark red — ARI = 1.00, runs landed on identical clusters. But two rows pop: runs 3 and 7 sit at ARI ≈ 0.72 and 0.76 against every other run. They found different local minima.
This is real-life clustering: most runs converge to the same answer, a few find different solutions, and you can’t tell from the algorithm output which solution is the “right” one. There’s no global ranking — they’re all local optima.
About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
sklearn’s defenses (and their limits)
# sklearn defaults:
KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, init='k-means++')

n_init=10 — runs k-means 10 times, keeps the lowest-inertia run
k-means++ — picks initial centroids that are spread apart, not random rows


helps. doesn’t fix it. different choices of random_state can still land on different solutions.

What sklearn does by default. n_init=10 is a major defense — out of 10 random starts, you keep the best one. k-means++ initialization picks initial centroids that are spread apart (algorithmic: pick the first centroid randomly; pick each subsequent centroid with probability proportional to squared distance to the nearest existing centroid). Together these dramatically reduce — but do not eliminate — sensitivity to the random seed.
The earlier heatmap used n_init=1 to expose the raw instability. With n_init=10, you’ll see fewer disagreements, but the underlying issue (multiple local minima) hasn’t gone away.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
what does “Cluster 1” mean?






Warning


if your clusters change when the seed changes, the labels carry no portable meaning.
“Cluster 1” in one run is not “Cluster 1” in the next — and may not be the same set of points at all.




→ never report cluster labels ('1', '2', '3') — report profiles (“budget”, “moderate”, “premium”)

The interpretation gotcha. K-means doesn’t label the clusters — it just produces them. The integer labels are arbitrary; sklearn assigns them in the order the algorithm happens to discover the centroids.
Practical rule: never put cluster_id = 1 in a report. Profile the cluster (avg price, room type, location) and name it for what it captures. “Budget shared rooms” is portable; “Cluster 1” is not.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
you have to ship something. it’s monday.
your host wants the three competitive segments by friday. elbow plot is gradual, silhouette peaks at k=2, two of the 10 runs disagree.
name two concrete things you’d do before reporting any of the clusters.


DISCUSSION: think-pair-share (5 min). 1 min think + 2 min pair + 2 min debrief.
Target answers (collect any 2): 1. Run with n_init=50 (or higher) and a fixed seed. Buys stability at the cost of compute. 2. Profile every cluster — mean feature values per cluster — and name them. Don’t report integer labels. 3. Try a different feature set and see if the clusters change qualitatively. Foreshadows block 3. 4. Visualize the clusters on at least two 2D slices (price vs. reviews, lat vs. lon, accommodates vs. price) — different views reveal different structure. 5. Decide whether k=3 matches what the host needs — three competitive tiers might be what they want regardless of the math. 6. Drop features you don’t trust (e.g., lat/lon if you already know boroughs from another column).
Key insight: the validation tools don’t pick k for you; they let you rule out bad choices. Beyond that, clustering is a judgment call you defend with profiles, not a formula you compute.
If a pair says “give up and use random forest” — fine but reframe: clustering is descriptive, not predictive. We’re not trying to predict anything; we’re trying to summarize structure.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text: your features pick your clusters

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
section: the same data, two feature sets
text:
the same data, two feature sets


with lat/lon
price, lat, lon, accommodates, bedrooms, reviews, availability, minimum_nights, room_type



without lat/lon
price, accommodates, bedrooms, reviews, availability, minimum_nights, room_type


drop two columns out of ten. same data, same k, same standardization.


how different can the clusters be?

Frame the experiment. We’re going to rerun k-means with one change: remove latitude and longitude. Everything else identical — same data, same k=3, same standardization, same algorithm.
Two columns out of ten. How big a difference can that make?
Predict-then-reveal — students should expect some difference, but the magnitude is the surprise.
About 30 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
the reveal


left: lat/lon in → clusters trace geography    right: lat/lon out → clusters mix across the map

The chapter’s central reveal. Two NYC maps, both colored by k-means cluster, both at k=3.
Left (with lat/lon): clusters are sharply separated on the map. Green covers Manhattan and central Brooklyn; the others split the rest. Geographically clean, because we handed the algorithm geography.
Right (without lat/lon): the three colors are mixed across the same map. Geography is no longer a primary axis — the clusters are organized around something else (price, room type, size).
The pedagogical moment: same data, same algorithm, same k, two columns difference — totally different story. Block 2’s elbow plot and silhouette score wouldn’t have told you which to prefer.
About 2 min. Let it sit.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
section: what the right-side clusters actually capture
text:
what the right-side clusters actually capture




cluster
size
avg price
avg bedrooms
top room type




0
13,438
$77
1.1
private room


1
15,053
$186
1.9
entire home/apt


2
651
$57
0.4
shared room






budget (shared room, low price, small)
moderate (private room, moderate price)
premium (entire apartment, high price, larger)



Profile the right-side clusters by their mean feature values. Three clean strata emerge: - Cluster 2 (~650 listings): shared rooms, ~$57/night — the budget end. - Cluster 0 (~13k): private rooms, ~$77 — moderate. - Cluster 1 (~15k): entire apartments, ~$186 — premium.
Reviews per listing are roughly similar across the three; the split is on room type and price. These are competitive sets a host can actually price against: “I’m in the moderate-private-room set; here’s what they charge.”
Compare to the left-side (with lat/lon) profile — which would have collapsed to “Manhattan-ish vs. Brooklyn-ish vs. outer-borough-ish.” The host already has that column.
About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
why did lat/lon dominate?

two features → spatial separation is strong and clean
a Manhattan listing and a Bronx listing are standard-deviations apart in (lat, lon)


every other feature gets drowned out


moral: if a feature carries clean geometric separation, it will dominate the cluster boundaries

Why lat/lon are so dominating. In NYC, the spatial spread (~0.4 degrees lat × 0.3 degrees lon) is large relative to typical within-borough variation, so once we standardize, a cross-borough listing pair is several standard deviations apart in (lat, lon). That swamps the more subtle separations in price, room type, etc.
The general principle: any feature whose values cleanly separate the data into geometric clumps will dominate k-means. ZIP code, neighborhood, even time-of-listing if the data spans years. These features can be useful for prediction but swamp clustering toward whatever they already encode.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
which features would you drop?
your host wants competitive segments for pricing. for each feature, decide: keep, drop, or it depends.

latitude, longitude
neighbourhood_group_cleansed (Manhattan / Brooklyn / …)
host_id
last_review_date
description text length

defend each choice in one sentence.


DISCUSSION: think-pair-share (5 min). 2 min think + 2 min pair + 1 min debrief.
Target answers: - lat/lon: drop — we just saw this. The host already knows the borough; including geography makes clustering rediscover it. - neighbourhood_group_cleansed: drop (same reason as lat/lon, just discrete). If you really want geography, model it separately by stratifying. - host_id: drop — identifier, not a feature. Including it would create one-cluster-per-host nonsense. - last_review_date: it depends — could indicate activity/staleness (useful) or time-since-listing-creation (less useful). At minimum convert to days-since. - description text length: it depends — could proxy effort, listing quality, or just verbosity. Worth a try in a robustness pass.
Key insight: feature selection IS the cluster-design step. The algorithm doesn’t decide; you do. Defend each feature by what segment-relevant information it carries.
This is the lecture’s central skill. The math is twenty lines of Python. The thinking is here.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
genes mirror geography
PCA on 197,000 SNPs from 1,387 Europeans. no geographic info given to the algorithm.

it drew us a map of Europe



PC1 vs. latitude: r^2 = 0.71
PC2 vs. longitude: r^2 = 0.72
50% of people placed within 310 km of birthplace



math result: PCA on spatial data generically produces these patterns when similarity decays with distance
Novembre et al., Nature 2008; Novembre & Stephens, Nature Genetics 2008

The canonical case. Novembre et al. 2008 ran PCA on ~197K SNPs across 1,387 individuals from across Europe. They handed the algorithm no geographic information whatsoever — just the genotypes. The first two principal components, when plotted, reproduced the map of Europe.
The temptation is to read this as “the algorithm discovered hidden population structure.” The same research community published the math showing otherwise: Novembre & Stephens 2008 (Nature Genetics) proved that PCA on any spatial data with distance-decaying similarity will produce gradient and sinusoidal patterns generically — no historical demographic event required. François et al. 2010 went further: under range-expansion simulations, PC1 can orient perpendicular to the true expansion direction.
So the famous Cavalli-Sforza interpretation of European PC1 as “Neolithic farmers walking west” is unidentified by this method — the gradient is built into PCA on spatial data, not into European prehistory.
Consequence: these maps are routinely misappropriated to support biological-race claims, which the actual authors explicitly reject. The “clusters” reflect geographic sampling structure and continuous clines, not discrete groups.
90-second story for class: “We gave the algorithm DNA, with zero geographic info. It drew us a map. Did it discover hidden populations? Or did it rediscover the obvious — your neighbors share more ancestry with you than strangers do? The math says the second.”
The bridge to lec15: same algorithm family (k-means and PCA both partition by Euclidean distance after standardization), same lesson. Feature set in → structure out, and the structure mostly reflects what we put in.
About 2 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
predictive policing finds the police
Lum & Isaac, 2016 — PredPol replication on Oakland 2010 drug arrest data

predicted hotspots: West Oakland + International Blvd ~200× more drug arrests there than elsewhere


true drug use (NSDUH survey): roughly uniform across the city



black residents would be targeted at roughly twice the rate of whites — despite roughly even drug use

Lum & Isaac, Significance 2016

Same lesson, much higher stakes. Lum and Isaac reimplemented PredPol — a real predictive-policing product deployed in Santa Cruz, LAPD, and others — on Oakland’s 2010 drug arrest records. PredPol’s only feature was where past arrests happened.
The algorithm’s hotspots concentrated in West Oakland and along International Boulevard: about 200× the arrest density of “non-hotspot” areas. They then compared to estimated true drug use (from the National Survey on Drug Use and Health combined with Census demographics) — which is roughly uniform across Oakland.
The clusters traced arrest patterns, not crime patterns. Because that’s the feature the algorithm got. Feed the model “where we arrest people,” and it will tell you “arrest more people there.” The feedback loop closes itself: predict a hotspot → patrol there → more arrests → tomorrow’s model more confident.
Quantitative consequence the paper drove home: under PredPol, Black residents would face about 2× the policing exposure of white residents, residents of other non-white races about 1.5×, despite roughly even underlying drug use across groups.
Policy consequence: Santa Cruz banned predictive policing in 2020 (first US city), LAPD ended its contract that year. This paper is the central empirical citation in the AI fairness literature.
For class: “Same algorithm, two feature sets. Train on arrests, you get a map of where we already police. Train on a public-health survey, you get a map of where drugs are. Different maps. The choice between them is a policy decision in statistics clothing.”
About 2 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
section: sixteen personality types — or four coin-flips at the median
text:
sixteen personality types — or four coin-flips at the median
MBTI assigns each test-taker to one of 16 “types” by binarizing four trait scores at zero.







Warning


the underlying scores are roughly normal, with the mode at the cutpoint






~50% of takers get a different type on retest within weeks
$20M/yr industry; used by 89 of the Fortune 100



the “types” are the bars to the left and right of one arbitrary line through a bell curve
McCrae & Costa, J. Personality 1989; Bess & Harvey, J. Personality Assessment 2002

The cleanest demonstration of feature-induced clusters: the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator. MBTI assigns each test-taker to one of 16 personality types via four binary axes (E/I, S/N, T/F, J/P). The procedure is: score each axis on a continuous scale, then cut at zero. Above zero → letter A; below → letter B.
McCrae & Costa 1989 (J. of Personality) and Bess & Harvey 2002 (J. of Personality Assessment) showed: the underlying scores on each axis are roughly normally distributed, with the mode (most common score) sitting right at the cutoff. So the “types” are produced by cutting a unimodal continuum at its densest point — guaranteeing that small score perturbations re-classify the person. About 50% of test-takers get a different type on retest within a few weeks.
Bess & Harvey specifically debunked earlier “bimodality” reports as artifacts of BILOG default quadrature settings: when finer numerical settings were used, the score distributions became strongly center-weighted, not bimodal.
So MBTI’s 16 types are an imposed structure, not a discovered one. The clusters exist because the test designer wrote them in.
Consequence: ~$20M/year industry; used by 89 of the Fortune 100 in hiring, team-building, career counseling. Decisions made on the basis of a clustering structure the data doesn’t actually have.
Cross-domain pattern: in three independent fields — genetics, policing, psychology — the same lesson recurs because clustering’s first job is to mirror its inputs, not to discover something independent of them.
About 2 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
same lesson, three fields

genetics: PCA on SNPs → map of Europe (geography, not biology)
policing: cluster on arrest locations → map of policing (not crime)
psychology: binarize trait scores → 16 types (not real categories)
Airbnb (today): cluster with lat/lon → boroughs (not market segments)


clustering’s first job is to mirror its inputs, not to discover something independent of them

The synthesis slide. Four examples, one mechanism. Whatever feature set you hand the algorithm dominates the clusters you get.
This is the deepest version of the lecture’s lesson. Students should leave with the instinct: before I trust any cluster summary, I ask what features were fed in and what they encode.
About 60 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
the clustering gotcha checklist
before reporting any cluster, ask:

what features did I include? — geography, IDs, dates often dominate
what features did I drop, and why? — feature choice is the model
is k defensible? — elbow + silhouette + business reason
are the clusters stable? — n_init=50, multiple seeds, ARI > 0.9
can I name each cluster from its profile? — if not, you have an artifact
does the clustering answer a question someone has? — if no, don’t ship


Six-item checklist. This is the chapter’s “what an uncritical analysis skips” list, reframed as the questions a careful analyst asks before reporting.
Order matters: features first (the biggest lever), then k, then stability, then interpretation, then decision-relevance. Each one is a single sentence that, if asked, would catch a common failure.
About 90 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
demo: clustering in the notebook

colab.research.google.com/…/lec15-clustering.ipynb

what to watch:

toggle lat/lon in the feature set — watch the clusters flip
sweep n_init from 1 to 50 — watch ARI agreement climb
try k = 2, 3, 5, 8 — read the profiles, not the labels


Demo slide. Notebook has the full pipeline; the three knobs above are the experiments worth running live or as homework.
If running short: skip the notebook in lecture, point at it as homework.
About 30 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
summary

k-means — assign, recompute, repeat; minimizes inertia \sum_k \sum_{i \in C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2
standardize — same lesson as PCA, Euclidean distance can’t see units
elbow + silhouette — sanity checks, not oracles
non-deterministic — different seeds, different clusters; use n_init + ARI to check stability
your features pick your clusters — the biggest lever, and the analyst’s responsibility


Five takeaways, in the order they were covered.
The fifth — “your features pick your clusters” — is the headline. If a student takes one thing from the lecture, this is it.
About 1 min.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
all models are wrong, but some are useful


George Box. The clusters aren’t “true” categories hiding in the data. They’re a simplification we built — and the question to ask is whether that simplification helps someone make a better decision.
In our case: yes, if we drop lat/lon and report market segments to the host. No, if we hand the borough column back to them.
About 15 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
next: when validation isn’t enough
we’ve trained models, tested them, validated them.

what happens when the world changes after we deploy?



temporal leakage — random splits leak future information
feedback loops — predictions change the outcome
Goodhart’s law — every target becomes a target


Forward pointer to Ch 16. We’ve spent the course building and validating models. Ch 16 asks the question those tools can’t answer: what breaks when the data changes? Three failure modes that don’t show up in any train/test split.
The setup question: random train/test only works if past data looks like future data. What if it doesn’t?
About 45 sec.

title: Lecture 15: Clustering — K-Means
text:
feedback






forms.gle/feedback




what worked? what didn’t? what’s still confusing?




Standard feedback slide. Same form as previous lectures.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & Visualization — Facilitation Script
text:
Total time: 80 minutes (73 planned + 7 buffer) Notebook: Colab link Chapter: Book chapter 2 (lec02-eda.qmd)




Open the Colab notebook and clear all outputs
Test that the notebook runs (hospital data loads, Airbnb data loads)
Have the slide deck open in presenter view
Check that the feedback QR code link works







Slide: “concept check”
Display the ERR question. Give 15 seconds of silent thought.
The answer: 1.05 is about 1.3 SDs above the mean (1.05 - 1.01 = 0.04, divided by 0.03 ≈ 1.3). Notable but not extreme. If students say “yes, flag it” — push back: “one standard deviation isn’t that unusual. What if the SD were 0.01 instead?” This sets up distributional thinking.



Slides: “the perils of summary statistics” → scatter reveal
Show the table first. Read aloud: “four datasets, identical mean, SD, correlation, regression line.” Pause. “So they must look the same, right?” Advance to the scatter plots. Let the image land for 5 seconds before speaking. “Same statistics. Completely different data. This is the single most important lesson in EDA.”



Slide: “same stats, different graphs”
“The modern update. All 12 datasets have the same mean, SD, and correlation — to two decimal places. One of them is a dinosaur.” Let the GIF loop once. Move on.



Slide: “today”
Read the three items. “By the end of class, you’ll have a checklist for exploring any dataset, you’ll know what ‘typical’ means and when it lies, and you’ll understand how missing data can silently bias everything downstream.”







Slide: “always look at your data”
Cinematic pause. Don’t speak. Let the text sit for 3 seconds, then advance.



Slide: “the EDA checklist”
Walk through each step: “Shape first — how big is this thing? Types next — is everything what you expect? Then distributions — what does each variable look like? Missing data — how much is gone and why? Finally, relationships — how do variables relate?” Emphasize: “The order matters. Shape is cheap. Types catch problems early.”



Slide: “a toy example: five hospitals”
“Here’s a small piece of the hospital readmissions data. Take 10 seconds — what’s wrong with this table?” Wait. Students should spot “Too Few to Report” in the readmissions column. After the reveal: “Python will coerce this entire column to strings. Every function that expects numbers will silently fail or give wrong results.”



Slide: “Demo: EDA checklist on hospital data”
Switch to Colab notebook.
Cells to run (chapter 2, sections 2.1 through first histogram):

df.shape — “3,100+ rows, let’s see what we’ve got”
df.head() — “Real hospital names. Six conditions tracked.”
df.info() — Key moment: “Look at Number of Readmissions — it’s object, not int64. That’s our ‘Too Few to Report’ string forcing the whole column to strings.”
df.describe() — Key moment: “Count row for ERR shows ~2,800. But we just saw 3,100 rows. Remember this number — we’ll come back to it.”
Faceted histogram of ERR by condition — “Distributions look reasonable. Roughly symmetric, centered near 1.0. Everything seems fine.” Pause. “It’s not.”

Return to slides.



Slide: “what the demo showed”
Brief recap. Don’t belabor — students just watched it.



Slide: discussion slide — “df.describe() reports 2,800 values…”
Read the prompt aloud. “One minute to think on your own.”
After 1 min: “Turn to your neighbor. Where did the 300 rows go?”
After 2 more min: “Who wants to share?” Take 2-3 responses.
If stuck: “What does .describe() do with NaN values?”
Debrief: “.describe() silently drops NaN. 300 hospitals have missing data — and we didn’t notice until we checked the count against .shape. Lesson: always compare the count row to your total rows.”







Slide: “what does ‘typical’ mean?”
Cinematic pause.



Slide: “not all data is this friendly”
“Hospital ERR was well-behaved — symmetric, moderate tails. Not all data is this friendly. Let’s look at something messier.”



Slide: “Airbnb prices: a cleaning puzzle”
“29,000 Airbnb listings in New York City. You want to know what a typical listing costs. You load the data and run .mean() — and get a TypeError. Why?” Show the toy table. “What needs to happen before this column is numeric?” After the reveal: “Dollar signs, commas, the word ‘unknown’, a negative price. Real data is messy.”



Slide: “Demo: Airbnb price cleaning and histogram”
Switch to Colab.
Cells to run (chapter 2, Airbnb sections):

Data loading, check df['price'].dtype → object. “Just like the hospital data — strings where we expected numbers.”
Cleaning pipeline: regex strip, pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce'), replace negatives. “Three lines of code to go from strings to numbers.”
sns.histplot of cleaned prices. Key moment: “Look at that skew. Most listings cluster at $50-$200, but the tail stretches to nearly $1,000.”
Before computing mean/median, ask: “What do you think the mean is? The median? Which one is bigger?” Take a few guesses. Then compute. Mean ~$152, median ~$106. “The gap tells you the distribution is skewed.”

Return to slides.



Slide: “the histogram tells a different story”
“Dramatic right skew. Most listings are $50-$200, but a few luxury listings pull the tail way out. Which single number represents ‘typical’?”



Slide: “mean vs. median”
“We saw the histogram. Now the vocabulary.” Reveal mean definition. “The arithmetic average. Sensitive to extreme values — those $999 listings are pulling it right.” Reveal median. “The middle value. Half below, half above. Barely moves when you add expensive outliers.”



Slide: “which summary do you trust?”
“Mean $152, median $106. If you price your listing at the mean — $152 — roughly what fraction of listings are cheaper than yours?” Give 15 seconds. “Well over half. The right tail pulls the mean above where most of the data lives. The median is a better measure of typical.”



Slide: “quartiles and IQR”
“Quartiles divide the data into four equal parts. The IQR — Q3 minus Q1 — is the width of the middle 50%. It’s robust: extreme values barely change it. Mean and standard deviation are not robust.”



Slide: “same data, different lens”
“Two views of the same data. On the raw scale, everything’s bunched at the left and the tail is invisible. On the log scale, the distribution looks roughly symmetric. The log scale doesn’t change the data — it changes the ruler.”



Slide: “Demo: log scale and scatter plots”
Switch to Colab.
Cells to run:

ax.set_xscale('log') on the histogram. “Roughly symmetric on the log scale. The heavy tail becomes interpretable.”
Price vs. accommodates scatter: linear scale first (mess), then log y-axis (clear upward trend). “On the log scale, each additional guest roughly multiplies the price.”
If time: 2D histogram.

Return to slides.



Slide: discussion slide — “sketch what you expect”
“I’m about to plot price on a log y-axis versus number of guests on the x-axis. Here’s the key question: does each additional guest add a fixed dollar amount — say $50 — or multiply the price by a factor? If it’s additive, what does that look like on a log scale? If it’s multiplicative?”
Give 1 min to sketch or discuss.
After 1 min: show the scatter plot from the notebook.
Debrief: “It’s roughly linear on the log scale — meaning the relationship is multiplicative. Each additional guest multiplies the price by a roughly constant factor, maybe 1.3×. If it were additive, the curve would flatten on the log scale. The spread also increases — bigger groups have more price variability.”







Slide: “what’s missing?”
Cinematic pause.



Slide: “the 300 missing hospitals”
“Remember block 1? df.describe() counted 2,800 values, but there were 3,100 rows. Those 300 rows are hospitals whose readmission counts were ‘Too Few to Report.’ Let’s find out who they are.”



Slide: “suppression varies by condition”
“CMS suppresses the count when it’s too small. But the rates are wildly different: CABG — coronary artery bypass — about 50% suppressed. Heart failure — only about 8%.” Pause. “Which hospitals are most likely to have small counts?” Wait 5-10 seconds. “Small hospitals. Rural hospitals. Specialty centers. Hospitals with low patient volume for that condition.”



Slides: MCAR → MAR → MNAR (one slide each)
MCAR (1 min): “Missing completely at random. No pattern. Example: a lab tech drops 10% of blood samples. The destroyed samples aren’t systematically different from the rest. Safe to ignore — you lose power but not accuracy.”
MAR (1 min): “Missing at random — conditional on something we observe. Example: older patients skip a survey question, but we know their age. The missingness has a pattern, but we can see the pattern. We can sometimes correct for it.”
MNAR (2 min): “Missing not at random. The value itself determines whether you see it. Example: patients who had complications don’t respond to the follow-up survey. The outcome we want to measure is exactly what drives the missingness.” Pause. “This is the most dangerous pattern. Dropping these rows introduces bias — a systematic error that doesn’t shrink with more data.”



Slide: “classify this”
“The CMS data suppresses hospitals with small readmission counts. MCAR, MAR, or MNAR?” Give 10 seconds. Some students will say MAR. Push back: “Hospital size is observed, yes. But the censoring threshold is on the readmission count itself — the value we’re trying to measure. That’s MNAR.”



Slides: three simulation plots
Plot 1 — all hospitals: “Let’s build a toy example where we know the truth. 20 simulated hospitals. Small hospitals on the left — slightly higher, noisier readmission rates. The line marks the true mean.”
Plot 2 — censored: “Now suppress the 5 smallest hospitals. Grayed out — ‘Too Few to Report.’ The dashed line is the true mean. The solid red line is the reported mean — computed only from the 15 visible hospitals. The mean shifted down.”
Plot 3 — histograms: “Side by side. All 20 hospitals on the left. After censoring on the right. The distribution shifts. The reported mean underestimates the true mean. This is MNAR bias.”



Slide: “back to the real data”
“This is exactly what’s happening in the CMS data. But in the real world, we can’t peek behind the curtain. We know the bias exists. We can reason about its direction. But we can’t measure how big it is without assumptions.”



Slide: “Demo: who are the missing hospitals?”
Switch to Colab.
Cells to run (chapter 2, missing data sections):

df['Number of Readmissions'].isna().sum() — total missing count
Breakdown by condition — show the variation (CABG ~50%, HF ~8%)
Bar chart of suppression rates — “This matches what we said on the slide.”
Contingency table / heatmap: condition × suppression status
If available: characteristics of suppressed vs. non-suppressed hospitals (size, location, patient demographics)

Key moment: “CABG: half the hospitals invisible. Half the data we’d need to evaluate cardiac surgery quality — gone.”
Return to slides.



Slide: discussion slide — “does the reported average overestimate or underestimate?”
Read the setup aloud: “CMS averages ERR across reporting hospitals to set the penalty threshold. The 300 suppressed hospitals are disproportionately small and rural. Suppose their ERR tends to be above 1.0.” Then the question: “Does the reported average overestimate or underestimate the true national average? Who gets hurt?”
“One minute on your own.”
After 1 min: “Talk to your neighbor.”
After 2 more min: “Let’s hear it.” Take 2-3 responses.
If a student challenges the premise: “Good catch — ERR adjusts for patient severity. But it doesn’t adjust for hospital resources, staffing, or follow-up programs. Small hospitals may lack those, which is what drives ERR above 1.0 even after the patient-mix adjustment.”
If stuck: “If the missing hospitals would pull the average up, and they’re excluded, which way is the reported average biased?”
Debrief: “The reported average is too low. The penalty threshold, calibrated to this biased average, may be too lenient. And the hospitals most likely to need support — the small, rural ones — are the ones we can’t see.”







Slide: “association is not causation”



Slide: “students who attend office hours get higher grades”
“Students who attend office hours get higher grades. How much of the grade boost is from office hours — the practice, the feedback — and how much is from the kind of student who shows up?” Give 10 seconds.



Slide: “the confounder”
“Student motivation is the confounder. Motivated students go to office hours and study hard independently. Office hours probably do help — you get practice, feedback, clarification. But the raw correlation overstates how much. If the university mandated attendance, the grade boost would be smaller than the correlation suggests.” Pause. “When you see an association, always ask: what else could drive both variables? We’ll formalize this idea later in the course.”







Slide: “takeaways”
Map each bullet back to a specific moment: - “EDA workflow — remember the dtype discovery? Shape, types, distributions, missing data.” - “Distributions reveal what summaries hide — the mean was $152 but the typical listing was $106.” - “Missing data tells a story — those 300 hospitals aren’t random. They’re small, rural, and invisible.” - “An association can be real without being causal — office hours.”



Slide: “next time”
“We can describe and diagnose the data. Next time: how do we clean it? What do we do with the 300 missing hospitals?”



Slide: “before next class”
Point to Chapter 2, notebook exercises, HW1 due date. Brief.



Slide: one-minute feedback
“Last thing — anonymous feedback. QR code on the screen. Two questions: what was most useful, what was most confusing. Takes 30 seconds. This directly shapes how I teach the next class.”








Section
Duration
Cumulative




Opening
5 min
5 min


Block 1: EDA checklist
20 min
25 min


Block 2: Distributions
20 min
45 min


Block 3: Missing data
20 min
65 min


Confounding teaser
3 min
68 min


Closing
5 min
73 min


Buffer
7 min
80 min



If running long: Cut the second demo in block 2 (log scale, 3 min) — summarize verbally and show the plot as a static image instead. This buys 3 minutes.
If running short: Extend the MNAR discussion. Ask: “What would you do if you were CMS? How would you handle the missing hospitals?” This can absorb 3-5 extra minutes productively.













Demo
Chapter sections
Key cells
Time




EDA checklist
2.1 through first histogram
shape, head, info, describe, ERR histogram
5 min


Airbnb cleaning
