title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the perils of summary statistics

four datasets. identical mean, SD, correlation, regression line.

so they must look the same, right?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: Let this land. “Same statistics, completely different data.” This is the single most important lesson in EDA: numbers alone are not enough. You must visualize.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
same stats, different graphs


Matejka & Fitzmaurice, “Same Stats, Different Graphs” (Autodesk Research, 2017)


The modern update to Anscombe. All 12 datasets have the same mean, SD, and correlation to two decimal places. One is a dinosaur. Spend 15 seconds — the image does the work.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: always look at your data

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
today: the EDA workflow

first look: shape, types, first impressions
distributions: what does each variable look like?
relationships: how do variables relate?
categorical comparisons: groups and compositions
missing data: how much? any patterns?
confounding: when associations mislead


Six parts, following the book chapter exactly. Each one builds on the last. We’ll use one primary dataset — Airbnb listings — throughout.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
four modes of statistics

summary: describe the data (today)
prediction: forecast new values
inference: quantify uncertainty
causation: establish cause and effect


today we focus on summary — what’s in the data, and what traps are hiding in it

Brief framing. We’ll return to prediction, inference, and causation throughout the course. Today is about looking before you model.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 1: first look at the data

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the dataset: NYC Airbnb
29,142 listings from Inside Airbnb — every active rental in New York City

you’re a traveler trying to find a good deal. where do you start?

Set the scene. This is real data — not a cleaned textbook dataset. It has all the problems real data has.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: Demo: first look at Airbnb data
text:
Demo: first look at Airbnb data
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through chapter 2, Part 1.
Key cells to run: - df.shape → (29,142, 96) — that’s 96 columns! - Select 11 columns: name, neighbourhood_group_cleansed, neighbourhood_cleansed, room_type, price, bedrooms, beds, security_deposit, number_of_reviews, review_scores_rating, accommodates - df.dtypes → note that most are int64/float64, text columns are object - df.describe() → scan the count row: different counts for different columns (missing data!) - df.head() → first impressions
End with: “we have 29,000+ listings, 11 columns, and already some gaps in the count row.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
what the demo showed

29,142 rows, 96 columns → selected 11
dtypes: mix of int64, float64, object
.describe() count row: some columns have fewer entries — missing data


six semantic types: continuous, discrete, nominal, ordinal, text, identifier

The six semantic types from the book. Price is continuous, bedrooms is discrete, borough is nominal, review scores could be treated as ordinal. The key lesson: pandas dtype tells you how it’s stored, not what it means.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 2: distributions

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: price distribution
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through Part 2 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Histogram of all prices → bunched at left, invisible tail - Zoom to $1–$500 → reveals the shape: peak near $100, long right tail - Before computing: ask students to estimate the mean vs. median - Show the histogram with mean (red dashed) and median (orange) marked
Key moment: “Most listings are $50–$200, but a few are $999. Does the mean represent a typical listing?”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: what does “typical” mean?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: the histogram tells a different story
text:
the histogram tells a different story
dramatic right skew — long tail to $999/night

most listings: $50–$200
a few listings: $500+


which single number represents a “typical” listing?

This motivates the definitions that follow. Students have seen the histogram — now they need vocabulary to describe what they saw.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
mean vs. median


mean

\(\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\)

the arithmetic average
sensitive to extreme values





median

the middle value when sorted

half below, half above
barely affected by outliers



Show these AFTER the histogram. The definitions land because students already have a visual intuition for why the mean is misleading here.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
which summary do you trust?
the mean is $133. the median is $100.

if you price your listing at the mean, roughly what fraction of listings are cheaper than yours?


well over half — the right tail pulls the mean above most of the data

Give 15 seconds. The answer is well over half. This is the core lesson: when data is right-skewed, the mean overrepresents expensive listings. The median is a better measure of “typical.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
quartiles and IQR


quartiles

divide sorted data into four equal parts

Q1 (25th percentile), Q2 (median), Q3 (75th percentile)





interquartile range (IQR)

Q3 − Q1 — the width of the middle 50%. a robust measure of spread.

for Airbnb prices: Q1 = $67, Q3 = $165, IQR = $98

Robust = barely affected by extreme values. Mean and SD are not robust. Median and IQR are. When you have heavy tails, robust summaries are more reliable.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
same data, different lens


raw scale
histogram bunched at left, invisible tail
mean pulled far from center

log scale
roughly symmetric
structure in the tail becomes visible


use a log axis to look at skewed data; log transform to model it

The log scale doesn’t change the data — it changes the ruler. Each tick mark represents a multiplication (×10) rather than an addition (+100). The book shows three panels: linear histogram, log-axis with log-spaced bins, and log-transformed histogram.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 3: relationships between variables

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
I’m about to plot price (log scale, y-axis) vs. number of guests (x-axis)

sketch what you expect
does each additional guest add a fixed dollar amount, or multiply the price?
does the spread stay constant or grow?



DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min — 1 min sketch/discuss, then reveal). After 1 min, show the scatter plot from the demo. The key question is additive vs. multiplicative: if each guest adds $50, the relationship is linear on the raw scale but curved on the log scale. If each guest multiplies the price by 1.3×, the relationship is linear on the log scale. Most students will predict “goes up” but won’t distinguish additive from multiplicative. The reveal: it’s roughly linear on the log scale — multiplicative. The spread also increases with group size.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: scatter plots and 2D histograms
open in Colab

DEMO (~3 min). Walk through Part 3 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Price vs. accommodates scatter: linear scale (overplotted mess) → log scale on y-axis (clear upward trend) - 2D histogram: overplotted scatter vs. 2D histogram with log color scale - Key insight: most listings accommodate 1–4 guests at $50–$200/night
The 2D histogram is the tool for dense data — scatter plots fail when points pile up.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
when scatter plots fail
29,000 points stacked on top of each other — you can’t see the pattern

2D histogram: bin both axes, color by count

reveals that the overwhelming majority of listings are 1–4 guests at $50–$200
the expensive outliers are rare, not representative


Brief slide to anchor what the demo showed. The 2D histogram is one of the most useful and least-taught EDA tools.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: part 4: categorical variables

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
room types and boroughs


room types

Entire home/apt: 15,022
Private room: 13,469
Shared room: 651


boroughs

5 boroughs, 212 neighborhoods
Manhattan has the most listings



bar charts make these comparisons easy — pie charts don’t

Set up the pie chart critique. The numbers come directly from the book chapter.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: how to lie like Steve Jobs
text:
how to lie like Steve Jobs


Steve Jobs presenting US smartphone market share. Apple has 19.5% of the market, but the 3D perspective and positioning make it look comparable to RIM’s 39%. A bar chart would instantly show the truth. Pie charts mislead because humans are bad at comparing angles and areas — especially when 3D perspective distorts them further.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Demo: categorical comparisons
open in Colab

DEMO (~5 min). Walk through Part 4 of the chapter.
Key cells to run: - Bar chart of room type counts - Bar chart of top 10 neighborhoods - Pie chart vs. bar chart for neighborhoods (pie makes Williamsburg, Bed-Stuy, Harlem indistinguishable) - Dodged bar chart: room type by borough - Box plots: price by room type (entire homes ~2× private rooms) - Violin plots: price by borough (Manhattan has wider spread)
Key moment: the pie vs. bar comparison. “Can you tell which neighborhood has more listings — Williamsburg or Bed-Stuy? In the pie chart, they look identical. The bar chart makes it obvious.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
you’ve seen price distributions for the whole city

predict: do entire homes or private rooms have higher median price?
by how much? (2×? 5×? 10×?)
does the spread differ?



DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min — 30 sec think, 1 min pair, then reveal). Reveal: entire homes are roughly 2× private rooms in median price. Private room prices cluster tightly around $60; entire home prices spread from $100 to $300. The violin plot makes this especially clear — the private room violin is narrow and peaked, the entire home violin is wide and flat.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
comparing compositions
three ways to show room type by borough:

dodged bar chart → compare counts across room types
stacked bar chart → see total listings per borough
standardized bar chart → compare proportions


Manhattan has a higher proportion of entire homes than Brooklyn

The three bar chart types from the book. Each one answers a different question. The standardized chart is the one that reveals composition differences — and sets up the confounding story in Part 6.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: what’s missing?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
40% of security deposits are missing
11,827 out of 29,142 listings have no deposit listed

is that random, or does it mean something?

The book shows overlapping density histograms comparing prices for listings with vs. without a deposit. If the distributions look the same, missingness might be random. If they differ, the missingness is informative.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
three patterns of missing data


MCAR (missing completely at random)

missingness has no pattern — unrelated to any variable



MAR (missing at random)

missingness depends on observed variables



MNAR (missing not at random)

missingness depends on the unobserved value itself — the most dangerous pattern


Brief definitions here. Chapter 3 covers these in depth with the hospital data. For now: “MCAR is rare, MAR is manageable, MNAR introduces bias.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
a host lists a $500/night apartment but doesn’t fill in the security deposit field

is the deposit more likely to be high or low?
does this look like MCAR, MAR, or MNAR?
if we drop listings with missing deposits, what happens to our price statistics?



DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (4 min — 1 min think, 2 min pair, 1 min share). Key insight: expensive listings might skip the deposit field because they handle deposits differently (through a property manager, or via Airbnb’s built-in damage protection). If high-price listings are more likely to have missing deposits, dropping them would shift our price distribution downward. This is a possible MAR pattern — missingness depends on observed price.
If a student says “maybe hosts with no deposit are more casual” — that’s also a valid hypothesis. The point is to think about why data is missing, not just that it’s missing.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: MNAR in the wild: hospital readmissions
text:
MNAR in the wild: hospital readmissions






CMS suppresses hospitals with “too few” readmissions to report — the value itself determines whether you see it

Brief callback to the hospital data. The left panel shows all hospitals. The right panel shows what happens when the smallest hospitals are suppressed — the reported mean shifts. This is MNAR: the count drives the suppression, and we want to analyze counts. Full treatment in Chapter 3.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text: association is not causation

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
Manhattan vs. Brooklyn
median price: Manhattan $135, Brooklyn $90

Manhattan is $45/night more expensive


but is that comparing like with like?

Set up the confounding reveal. The overall gap is $45. But what happens when we control for room type?

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
control for room type




Manhattan
Brooklyn
gap




Entire home
$180
$140
$40


Private room
$85
$62
$23


Shared room
$60
$35
$25


Overall
$135
$90
$45




the overall gap ($45) exceeds every room-type gap ($23–$40)

This is a composition effect. Manhattan has a higher proportion of entire homes (the most expensive room type), which pulls up its overall median. When comparing “like with like” — entire homes to entire homes, private rooms to private rooms — the borough difference shrinks. The overall gap overstates the true effect of location on price.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
the confounder


confounder

a variable that affects both the treatment and the outcome

inflates (or deflates) the observed association beyond the true causal effect


room type drives both location distribution and price

Manhattan isn’t as much more expensive as it looks — room type is doing some of the work

Room type is the confounder. Manhattan has more entire homes, and entire homes cost more. If you don’t control for room type, you attribute the composition difference to the borough. This is a causal concept; we’ll formalize it in Act 3. For now: “when you see an association, ask what else could drive both variables.”

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
section: students who attend office hours get higher grades
text:
students who attend office hours get higher grades

how much of the grade boost is from office hours, and how much from the kind of student who shows up?


motivation drives both OH attendance and studying — the raw correlation overstates the causal effect

A second example to reinforce the pattern. The association is real. Office hours probably do help. But the observed effect overstates the true causal benefit, because the same motivation that drives students to attend also drives them to study harder independently.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
how to lie with data

what’s wrong with each of these?

Quick warm-up. Show the two bad viz slides back-to-back, giving 20-30 seconds each. Students call out what they see. No formal activity structure — just hands up.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
y-axis is inverted — 0 at top, 1,000 at bottom


Reuters / C. Chan, 2014. Data: Florida Dept. of Law Enforcement


The red fill evokes blood dripping down — visually powerful but deeply misleading. At first glance, the downward slope after 2005 looks like a decrease. In fact, the y-axis runs from 0 (top) to 1,000 (bottom), so downward means more deaths. Gun deaths in Florida roughly doubled after Stand Your Ground.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
dates are not in chronological order, but sorted by bar height to fake a downward trend


Georgia Dept. of Public Health, May 2020


The x-axis reads: 28Apr, 27Apr, 29Apr, 01May, 30Apr, etc. This is sorted by total case count (decreasing), not by date. The visual impression is “cases are declining” — but the dates are scrambled. When plotted chronologically, cases were roughly flat or rising. Georgia DPH later corrected the chart after public outcry.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
takeaways

EDA workflow: shape → types → distributions → relationships → missing data
distributions reveal what summaries hide: always plot; use log scale for heavy tails
missing data tells a story: ask why before dropping rows
an association can be real without being causal: confounders inflate observed effects


Map each bullet back to a specific moment from the lecture. EDA checklist → the first look at Airbnb. Distributions → mean vs. median. Missing data → the 40% missing deposits. Association ≠ causation → Manhattan vs. Brooklyn, office hours.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
next time
we can describe and diagnose the data

how do we clean it? what do we do with missing values, wrong types, messy text?

Forward pointer to lecture 3 (data munging). The questions raised today — missing deposits, messy data — become actionable in the next lecture.

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
before next class

read Chapter 2 in the course notes
try the notebook exercises on your own
HW 1 due next week

title: Lecture 2: EDA & visualization
text:
one-minute feedback



what was the most useful thing you learned today?
what was the most confusing?

give feedback




Last 1-2 minutes. Anonymous. QR code goes to the same form. Entry field pre-filled with “2” for lecture number.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
am I pricing this right?


A friend just put their first Brooklyn listing on Airbnb and texts: “am I pricing this right?” First instinct — charge the average. But the average knows nothing about the listing. Their place has two bedrooms; bigger places should cost more. Before you answer, you want a pricing model that reflects what you actually know about the listing. That’s what this chapter builds, in two stages: (1) ignore every feature and predict a single number (the mean), then (2) let the prediction respond to one feature (bedrooms) by fitting a line.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
a friend’s Brooklyn listing
text from a friend: “just listed my 2-BR on Airbnb — am I pricing this right?”

first instinct: charge the average


but the average knows nothing about their listing


bigger places should cost more — can we do better?

This is the motivating scenario for the whole chapter. Stage 1: predict with no features (the mean). Stage 2: predict with one feature (bedrooms). Along the way: vectors, norms, projection, R².

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
today

vectors in data: rows, columns, norms
predicting with no features: the mean
predicting with one feature: simple regression
how good is the fit?: \(R^2\) and correlation
regression to the mean


Quick agenda. Jump straight in.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: vectors in data

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
two views of a dataset



listing
bedrooms
bathrooms
price




1
1
1
100


2
2
1
150


3
3
2
250


4
2
2
200


5
1
1
120





row view: each listing is a point in \(\mathbb{R}^d\) — listing 3 = \((3, 2, 250)\)
column view: each feature is a vector in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — bedrooms = \([1, 2, 3, 2, 1]\)


Two complementary views. Rows → points → similarity between listings. Columns → vectors → what a model can combine. Both matter today.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
rows as points

nearby points = similar listings

Five listings plotted in bedroom/bathroom space. Listings 1 and 5 sit almost on top of each other. Listing 3 is farthest out.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
visualizing high-dimensional vectors
a column of \(n\) prices lives in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — too many dimensions to draw as an arrow

index plot: plot value vs listing index





black = price, red = bedrooms (sorted by price) — they move together

The index plot is how we’ll visualize n-dimensional column vectors. Sort listings by price, then plot both columns on the same axes. When two vectors rise and fall together across listings, they “point” in the same direction in R^n. This visual agreement is what the inner product will measure.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
difference vector
\[\text{listing 3} - \text{listing 1} = (3, 2) - (1, 1) = (2, 1)\]

tells us how they differ: +2 bedrooms, +1 bathroom


but how far apart as a single number?

The difference vector captures direction and magnitude of change. To collapse to a scalar, we need the norm.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
norm = length of a vector


Norm

\(\|v\| = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + \cdots + v_n^2}\)
Pythagorean theorem in \(n\) dimensions.


np.linalg.norm([2, 1])
# sqrt(4 + 1) = 2.24


distance = norm of the difference: \(d(u, v) = \|u - v\|\)

The norm is just a generalized Pythagorean theorem. Distance between two vectors = norm of their difference.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: the norm will measure prediction error
text:
the norm will measure prediction error
predict every listing with a single number \(\widehat{y}\)

residual: \(\epsilon_i = y_i - \widehat{y}\) — one error per listing







\(\|\epsilon\|\) measures total prediction error

Preview of the geometry. Vertical segments from black dots (actual prices) to the green horizontal line (a constant prediction) are the residuals. Their norm is one number measuring how wrong we are overall. Our job is to make it small.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what does it mean for two Airbnb listings to be “similar”?


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min — 30 sec think, 1 min pair, then share). Think: which features should count? Pair: do you agree on the same features? Share: what’s your definition of “close”? Prompt: “what does it mean for two listings to be similar?” If stuck: “if one is $100 and the other is $500, are they similar? what if the features match perfectly?” Key insight: “similar” depends on what you care about. Distance in feature space is one definition — but it weights every feature equally, and features with big numeric ranges dominate. This is why we’ll later normalize.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: predicting with no features

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the simplest predictor: a constant
\(n\) prices. what single number \(\widehat{y}\) should we guess for every listing?







Before any features, the simplest thing we can do: predict the same number for every listing. What should that number be?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
two natural losses
minimize absolute error or squared error?






absolute → median; squared → mean

Left panel: sum of absolute deviations has V-shape, minimum at the median. Right panel: sum of squared errors is parabolic, minimum at the mean. Try the interactive widget in the book to explore this.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
why should the mean win?
before the proof, a moment of reflection:

as \(c\) moves through the data, what happens to the derivative of \((y_i - c)^2\)?


why does that force a balance exactly at \(\bar{y}\)?

Reflection prompt before the calculus. Give students 20-30 seconds to think about why squared error pulls toward the mean. Each squared term contributes a linear derivative in c — summing them gives a linear function that crosses zero at the average. This is the intuition the proof formalizes.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: why squared error gives the mean
text:
why squared error gives the mean
\[\frac{d}{dc}\sum_i (y_i - c)^2 = -2\sum_i (y_i - c) = 0\]

\[\Longrightarrow \quad c = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i = \bar{y}\]


the mean is the optimal constant predictor under squared error

Two-line calculus proof. Derivative is linear in c, so there’s a unique zero — at the mean. Later this same argument gives us the conditional mean: within any region of x, squared-error regression predicts the average of y in that region.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
notation


Regression notation


\(y = (y_1, \ldots, y_n)\) — response vector (actual prices)
\(\widehat{y}\) — prediction vector
\(\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i\) — sample mean
\(\epsilon = y - \widehat{y}\) — residual vector
\(\beta_0\) — intercept


for the constant model: \(\widehat{y}_i = \beta_0\), and the best \(\widehat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y}\)

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
residuals sum to zero
when \(\widehat{y}_i = \bar{y}\):
\[\sum_i \epsilon_i = \sum_i (y_i - \bar{y}) = n\bar{y} - n\bar{y} = 0\]

in vector notation:
\[\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0\]


the residual is orthogonal to the ones vector — our first normal equation

Remember this pattern — it generalizes. The best prediction makes the residual orthogonal to every feature used in the model. For the constant model, the only “feature” is the ones vector.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
squared error → conditional mean
same argument, applied within each slice of \(x\):
squared-error regression estimates \(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]\)

(absolute error → conditional median; other losses → other summaries)

This is one of the most important properties of squared loss: the regression function estimates the conditional mean. The “best line” passes through the conditional means of y given x.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
when should you predict with the mean vs the median?

real estate prices: mean or median?
insurance claims: mean or median?
what’s the difference between “typical” and “average”?



DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min — turn to your neighbor for 2 min, then share). Prompt: “mean vs median — when does each win?” If stuck: “which is sensitive to outliers? which gives the right answer for a total cost estimate?” Key insight: Median is robust to outliers and answers “what’s a typical listing?” Mean answers “if I rent out every listing, what’s the average revenue per listing?” Squared error cares about total cost — you can’t ignore the $999 listings. This is why regression uses squared error, not absolute error.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: predicting with one feature

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the mean ignores bedrooms

a 5-bedroom apartment and a studio get the same prediction

The mean gets the overall level right but misses the upward trend. Can we do better with bedrooms?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: a linear combination of two columns
text:
a linear combination of two columns
\[\widehat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, x_i\]

\(\widehat{y}\) is a linear combination of \(\mathbf{1}\) and \(x\):
\[\widehat{y} = \beta_0 \, \mathbf{1} + \beta_1 \, x\]








The prediction is a weighted sum of two column vectors: the ones vector (scaled by the intercept) and the bedrooms vector (scaled by the slope). Different weights give different predictions.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the span: all reachable predictions


Span

\(\text{span}(\mathbf{1}, x) = \{\beta_0 \mathbf{1} + \beta_1 x : \beta_0, \beta_1 \in \mathbb{R}\}\)
all lines you could draw through the scatter plot


every \((\beta_0, \beta_1)\) traces a different line — which one is best?

Every choice of (β₀, β₁) traces a different line. The span of {1, x} is the set of all possible prediction vectors. Before we show candidate lines, we want students to commit to a criterion for “best.”

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
before you see any candidate lines: what would “best” look like?


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (2 min — 30 sec commit to a criterion, then reveal). Hint to deliver verbally: “passes through the middle of the cloud” is a starting point — make it precise enough that you could compute it. Prompt: “commit to a criterion for ‘best’ BEFORE seeing any lines.” If stuck: “close your left eye and imagine the vertical gaps from each point to a line.” Key insight: Most students land on ‘balances the gaps’ or ‘minimizes total error’ — that’s squared error, and it leads directly to projection. Now reveal the candidate lines.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
section: many lines are possible — which is best?
text:
many lines are possible — which is best?



red, blue: too steep at the low end
purple: slopes the wrong way
green: closer but still arbitrary



we need a principled criterion — and it turns out to be geometric

Now the reveal. Walk through why each is wrong. The point: eyeballing isn’t enough. We need a principled definition of “best” — and the geometric view (projection) will give it to us.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
switch to the column view
\(y\), \(\mathbf{1}\), and \(x\) all live in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) — one coordinate per listing

\(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\) = a flat plane floating in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)


every line you could draw = one point on that plane


the actual price vector \(y\) sits off the plane — no line fits every listing perfectly

Before showing the projection cartoon, get students to picture the column view. n listings → vectors in R^n. Span of two columns = a 2D plane inside that high-dimensional space. Every regression line is a point on the plane. The data vector y lives off the plane. Now the question becomes: which point on the plane is closest to y?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the geometric view: projection


\(y\) is a point in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
\(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\) is a plane through the origin
the closest point in the plane is the best prediction
the residual \(\epsilon\) is perpendicular to the plane


Here’s the geometric heart. y is a point. The span of the features is a plane. The prediction ŷ lives in the plane — it’s a linear combination of the columns. The best ŷ is the point in the plane closest to y. Shortest distance from a point to a plane = perpendicular. That perpendicularity is the defining property of least squares.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the inner product


Inner product (dot product)

\(u^T v = \sum_{i=1}^n u_i v_i\)

positive → same direction
zero → orthogonal
negative → opposite directions



take \(u = (1, 0)\) along the x-axis:

\(u \cdot (1, 1) = 1\) → acute angle
\(u \cdot (0, 1) = 0\) → right angle
\(u \cdot (-1, 0) = -1\) → opposite



price and bedrooms both trend up → large positive \(y^T x\)

The inner product makes “perpendicular” precise. Zero inner product = orthogonal. The small 2D examples ground the rule: sign of the inner product tracks the angle. Callback to the index plot: if two columns rise and fall together, their inner product is positive and large.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
orthogonality = best fit
the best prediction makes the residual orthogonal to every feature:
\[\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad x^T \epsilon = 0\]


first: residuals sum to zero (same as the mean model!)
second: residuals have no remaining linear pattern with \(x\)



if some feature still aligned with the residual, you could reduce error by adjusting its coefficient

Two orthogonality conditions pin down the unique best line. The principle is the same as for the mean, just one more condition. If x^T ε were nonzero, we could absorb some of that pattern into the slope — so at the optimum, it must be zero.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
normal equations


Normal equations (simple regression)

\(\mathbf{1}^T \epsilon = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad x^T \epsilon = 0\)
where \(\epsilon = y - \beta_0 \mathbf{1} - \beta_1 x\)
— the algebraic form of “residual orthogonal to features”

these two equations determine the unique \((\widehat{\beta}_0, \widehat{\beta}_1)\)

Two equations, two unknowns. The normal equations are just the orthogonality conditions, written out. Every regression problem reduces to solving a system like this.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
the projection picture

\(\widehat{y}\) = orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto \(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\)
the right angle is why it’s the best fit

Clean version of the diagram. Perpendicular = shortest. This is the defining property of least squares.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
simple regression on Airbnb
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(df[['bedrooms']], prices)

28,778 listings


ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms
R² = 0.156


\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]

The full NYC Airbnb dataset, filtered to prices in [$10, $500]. One feature, closed-form solution via the normal equations. R² = 0.156 — bedrooms alone explain about 16% of the variation.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
interpreting the coefficients
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]


intercept $71: predicted price for a studio (0 bedrooms)
slope $49: each additional bedroom → $49 more per night



association, not causation — larger listings differ in many ways

The intercept is the base price of a studio. The slope says each additional bedroom is associated with $49 more per night. But bigger apartments differ in many ways from smaller ones — location, amenities, quality. We can’t say “adding a bedroom causes $49.” We’ll revisit this seriously in Chapter 18.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
plug in a few listings
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms}\]




listing
prediction




studio
$71


1-bedroom
$120


3-bedroom
$217


5-bedroom
$314





back to our friend’s 2-BR: model predicts $169/night

Concrete numbers make the model tangible. Plug bedrooms into the formula and read off predictions. For the 2-BR: 71 + 49 × 2 = 169. Our friend now has a starting anchor — though we’ll refine it with more features in Ch 5.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
verify orthogonality
y_hat = model.predict(df[['bedrooms']])
residuals = prices - y_hat
np.dot(residuals, np.ones(len(df)))     # ≈ 0
np.dot(residuals, df['bedrooms'].values) # ≈ 0

residuals · 1       :  -0.0000  ✓
residuals · bedrooms:  -0.0000  ✓


the errors have no linear pattern left that bedrooms could capture

Both dot products are effectively zero — the normal equations are satisfied. This is guaranteed by the math. What is NOT guaranteed is that residuals look like random noise — we’ll check that with residual plots in Chapter 5.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what would a curved pattern in the residual plot mean?


DISCUSSION: Think-pair-share (3 min — 30 sec think, 1 min pair, then share). Think: if residuals vs bedrooms showed a U-shape… Pair: what’s the model missing? Share: how would you fix it? Prompt: “what does a curved residual plot tell us?” If stuck: “the orthogonality condition says residuals are uncorrelated with bedrooms linearly. What kind of pattern would still satisfy that?” Key insight: A U-shape would mean the true relationship is nonlinear — a quadratic term would help. Forward pointer to feature engineering (Ch 5): we can add bedrooms² as a new column and the same orthogonality machinery still works.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: how good is the fit?

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2\): fraction of variance explained


\(R^2\) (coefficient of determination)

\[R^2 = 1 - \frac{\|\epsilon\|^2}{\|y - \bar{y}\|^2}\]

\(R^2 = 0\): no better than predicting \(\bar{y}\)
\(R^2 = 1\): perfect fit



for our Airbnb regression: \(R^2 = 0.156\)

R² compares the model’s error to the baseline of always predicting the mean. Bedrooms alone explain about 16% of the price variation — the other 84% will need more features.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
correlation


Correlation (Pearson’s \(r\))

\(r(u, v) = \dfrac{(u - \bar u)^T(v - \bar v)}{\|u - \bar u\|\,\|v - \bar v\|}\)
inner product of centered, length-normalized vectors.



\(-1 \le r \le +1\)
\(r = 0\) → centered vectors are orthogonal
unitless: rescaling \(u\) or \(v\) doesn’t change \(r\)


Center each vector (subtract its mean), normalize to unit length, take the inner product. That’s correlation. It measures the angle between the centered vectors — pure direction, no units. Rescaling or shifting either input leaves r unchanged.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2 = r(y, \widehat{y})^2\)

Pythagorean theorem (centered \(y\)):
\[\|y - \bar{y}\|^2 = \|\widehat{y} - \bar{y}\|^2 + \|\epsilon\|^2\]

\[R^2 = \frac{\|\widehat{y} - \bar{y}\|^2}{\|y - \bar{y}\|^2} = r(y, \widehat y)^2\]
the first ratio is the correlation between \(y\) and \(\widehat{y}\), by definition

After centering, y, y-hat, and the residual form a right triangle with y as the hypotenuse. R² is the squared ratio of the leg (centered y-hat) to the hypotenuse (centered y) — and that ratio is exactly the correlation r(y, y-hat) between centered y and centered y-hat, by definition. R² measures how much of y lives in the feature span.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
\(R^2 = r^2\) in simple regression
r = df['price'].corr(df['bedrooms'])
# r = 0.3944
model.score(df[['bedrooms']], prices)
# R² = 0.1556 = r²

for one predictor: \(R^2\) is literally \(r\) squared
(with multiple predictors, only \(R^2\) applies)

0.3944² ≈ 0.1556. R² = r² is only true for simple regression. With multiple predictors there’s no single r to square — we have to compute R² directly.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text: regression to the mean

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
Galton’s heights
Francis Galton, 1886: tall parents → tall children, but less tall






934 children, 205 families: \(r \approx 0.50\), slope \(\approx 0.71\) (Galton’s own estimate: 2/3)

Galton’s actual 1886 dataset — 934 adult children from 205 families. He multiplied female heights by 1.08 to put everyone on a common male scale, then plotted each child against the “midparent height” (average of father and 1.08 × mother). The regression line is flatter than the 45-degree line (child = midparent). Parents 2 SD above average have children predicted to be only about r × 2 SD above average — pulled toward the mean. Galton himself estimated the slope at about 2/3, which is close to what we find. This is a purely statistical phenomenon, not biological. For the full story, see Salsburg, The Lady Tasting Tea, Ch. 4.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
why “regression”?
since \(|r| < 1\), predicted \(y\) is always closer to \(\bar{y}\) (in SDs) than \(x\) is to \(\bar{x}\)

Galton called this “regression toward mediocrity” — the name stuck


purely statistical, not biological or causal

The phenomenon comes from imperfect correlation, not from any physical force pulling things toward the average. Extreme values are partly signal, partly luck — and luck doesn’t persist.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
where else do you see regression to the mean?

a team wins 90% of games one season. next year?
a student scores in the 99th percentile. next exam?
a player scores 45 points (season avg: 25). next game?

why does this keep happening?


DISCUSSION: Predict-then-reveal (3 min). Prompt: “regression to the mean in the wild” If stuck: “how much of an extreme outcome is skill vs luck?” Key insight: Any time performance is skill + noise, the best predictor after an extreme event is somewhere between the extreme and the long-run average. Coaches who bench a player after one bad game, or overpay after one great game, are ignoring this. The “Sports Illustrated jinx” is regression to the mean, not a curse.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
skill + luck, luck resets
90% win rate = part skill, part luck

next season: the skill carries over, the luck resets


99th percentile exam = real knowledge + a run of favorable questions


knowledge holds; favorable run does not


whenever \(|r| < 1\), extremes on one measurement look less extreme on the next

The mechanism in every case is the same: observed performance = underlying ability + noise. The ability carries over; the noise doesn’t. Not “the team got worse” — just that an unusual streak has no reason to persist. Same logic for exam scores, stock picks, player performance, business quarters. This is not a curse, not a jinx — just algebra.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
key takeaways

two views of data: rows = points, columns = vectors in \(\mathbb{R}^n\)
the mean is the best constant predictor under squared error
simple regression is the orthogonal projection of \(y\) onto \(\text{span}\{\mathbf{1}, x\}\)
residual ⊥ features: the defining property of least squares
\(R^2 = r(y, \widehat{y})^2\): how much of \(y\) lives in the feature span
regression to the mean: extremes don’t persist


The conceptual chain: vectors → mean → simple regression → projection → R². Each step adds one feature and one orthogonality condition.

title: Lecture 4: From the Mean to Simple Regression
text:
what we can’t answer yet
\[\widehat{y} = 71 + 49 \times \text{bedrooms} \qquad R^2 = 0.16\]


what about bathrooms? room type? neighborhood?
can more features make \(R^2\) bigger?
how do we encode categorical features?



next time: multiple regression (Chapter 5)

Forward pointer. Same orthogonality principle scales to any number of features. More columns → bigger span → closer projection. We’ll also transform raw data into features a linear model can use.

title: Lecture 14: PCA — Dimensionality Reduction — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 80 minutes Total slides: 37 (slide 1 = auto title from YAML) Primary dataset: S&P 100 daily log returns, 2014–2024 (95 stocks × ~2,770 trading days), plus sector metadata Hook: Portfolio analyst with 100 stocks on the desk. Boss asks: how many real dimensions of variation are there? Is “the market” really one number? Headline: Interpretability is not validation. Named factors are appealing — held-out portfolio variance is the proof. Two surprises: (1) standardize-or-not flips PC1 from “TSLA-dominated” to “the market factor”; (2) the scree elbow says k=1, but cross-validation picks k=3 — and that’s what wins on held-out variance. Cross-domain note: Pairs with lec15’s PredPol cautionary tale via the Novembre genetic-map case (slide 32). Structure: Opening (~3 min, slides 1–5) → Block 1: the picture + standardization (~22 min, slides 6–13) → Block 2: reading the components + the math (~22 min, slides 14–22) → Block 3: the payoff + cautionary tale (~28 min, slides 23–33) → Closing (~5 min, slides 34–37) → Buffer (~2 min)



Slide 1 (title): 30 sec.
Slide 2 (logistics): START RECORDING. 30 sec. Quiz 6 today (multiple testing + trees). HW 4 out Monday (PCA + clustering + Kaggle challenge). Project midterm reports due Friday May 15.
Slide 3 (the brief): 1 min. The decision frame: an analyst with 100 stocks trying to understand the structure. The boss’s question — “how many real dimensions?” — is exactly what PCA answers. The image is the chapter’s hook plot: five S&P 100 stocks (AAPL, JPM, XOM, KO, MSFT) over 2020. For most of January and February the lines drift apart. In mid-March they all crash together (red dashed = COVID). By summer they diverge — tech up, energy lagging.
Hold the punchline (PC1 explains ~40% of variance) for block 1.
Slide 4 (today): 30 sec. Three blocks: the picture (geometry), reading the components (interpretation + math), the payoff (portfolios in \(p > n\)).
Slide 5 (bridge from supervised): 1 min. Everything since Ch 4 has been supervised — there was always a \(y\). PCA is our first unsupervised method: no \(y\), no labels. The job is to find structure already in the features. K-means (next lecture) is also unsupervised — they’re the unsupervised pair.






Pearson 1901 + JPM/BAC scatter ground students in the geometry. Then the chapter’s two-stocks-by-hand computation makes the algebra concrete before we scale to 95. The standardization story (raw vs standardized PC1) is the first surprise: feature scaling matters, and skipping it lets TSLA dominate.
Slide 6 (dark transition): “the picture: best line, best subspace.”
Slide 7 (Pearson 1901): ~90 sec. PCA is over a century old. Karl Pearson asked the question in 1901 and answered it in any number of dimensions with elementary geometry. The figure on the left is from his original Phil. Mag. paper: a line \(AB\) through a cloud of points, with perpendicular drops \(p_i\). PCA picks \(AB\) to minimize \(\sum p_i^2\). That’s the whole problem. Eigenvalues and SVD come in as the answer, not the question.
Spend a moment on the figure — students recognize what it shows immediately.
Slide 8 (JPM/BAC scatter): ~2 min. Same Pearson picture, drawn on real data. Each blue dot is one trading day’s pair of returns: JPM on x, BAC on y, 2014–2024. Two big banks; correlation 0.89.
The cloud is elliptical. PC1 (orange) is the long axis — the shared up/down move of the two banks, the “banking sector” direction. PC2 (green) is the short axis — what’s left, the small idiosyncratic difference.
The dotted red line shows one day’s perpendicular drop onto PC1 — its squared length is what PCA minimizes, summed over all days.
Slide 9 (PC1 by hand): ~90 sec. The whole computation in seven lines. Two banks, two PCs, no mystery. - PC1 direction = (+0.65, +0.76) — both positive, both around 0.7. Northeast in the JPM-BAC plane. The “banking sector” direction in numbers. - PC1 variance share = 94.4% of total variance lives along PC1. PC2 picks up the rest. - Example day score = +6.12. The day with JPM up 6%, BAC up 3% projects far out along PC1 — almost the whole move is sector co-movement.
Scaling to 95 stocks doesn’t change the recipe — only the size of the matrix.
Slide 10 (scaling up): ~30 sec. The 95-stock dataset. 2,770 trading days × 95 surviving S&P 100 names. Same eigendecomposition, larger matrix.
Slide 11 (DISCUSSION: poll — predict which stocks dominate without standardization): 2 min. POLL. Four options: - (a) the largest companies (Apple, Microsoft, Berkshire) - (b) the most volatile stocks (Tesla, semiconductors) - (c) the financials (banks, insurance) - (d) PC1 will be roughly equal across all 95
Target: (b). PCA finds whichever direction has the largest raw variance. Tesla swings 5% on a slow day; KO barely moves 1%. Without standardization, the high-vol names dominate.
If a student picks (a): natural confusion (large cap → large index weight). PCA doesn’t see market caps, just variance. If they pick (d): correct after standardization, not before.
Slide 12 (without standardization — the contrast plot): ~2 min. Both panels show PC1 loadings sorted. Left (raw): steep nose, long flat tail. Top five most volatile names hold ~12% of PC1’s squared mass — over 2× the uniform baseline (5/95 ≈ 5.3%). PC1 is shaped by “TSLA and the other big movers”. Right (standardized): broad, uniformly positive bar. Every stock contributes nearly equally. Same data, same algorithm — different scale.
Slide 13 (the fix — standardize): ~1 min. Definition box for z-score. After standardization, PCA finds structure in the correlations instead of the magnitudes. For returns, this is almost always what we want. Mathematical statement: PCA on raw centered data decomposes the covariance matrix; PCA on standardized data decomposes the correlation matrix.







Scree plot, named-factor reveal (PC1/2/3), then the variational + SVD math. End the block with a discussion that surfaces the chapter’s central methodological lesson — interpretability is not validation.
Slide 14 (dark transition): “reading the components.”
Slide 15 (scree plot): ~90 sec. Left: bar chart, PC1 alone is ~40% of variance, sharp drop, long flat tail. Right: cumulative variance — 70% takes ~20 components, 80% takes ~35. A naive elbow read picks \(k=1\). Hold that — block 3 will show CV picks \(k=3\) instead. Different objectives, different answers.
The shape (“one big factor + long tail”) is the textbook signature of equity returns: market factor swamps everything, sector/style factors are smaller but real, the rest is idiosyncratic.
Slide 16 (elbow method — heuristic, not formula): ~1 min. Definition box for elbow method. The chapter’s empirical demo: scree elbow says \(k=1\), CV picks \(k=3\). The elbow ranks PCs by training variance — PC1 swamps the others. CV ranks by held-out portfolio variance — PC2/PC3 stabilize the covariance estimate enough to matter.
Moral: pick \(k\) by what wins on the task you care about.
Slide 17 (interpret PC1 — the market factor): ~2 min. Each PC is a vector of weights — the loadings.
Top panel (PC1): every loading is positive. A stock’s PC1 loading = its sensitivity to the market. PC1 is the textbook market factor.
Bottom panel (PC2, colored by GICS sector): sharp pattern. Purple bars (Financials — JPM, BAC, WFC, C, COF) positive. Red bars (Energy) positive. Green (Staples — PG, KO) and dark blue (Utilities) negative. PC2 is the cyclical-vs-defensive contrast. Cyclicals rally when economy strong; investors flee to staples/utilities for safety.
PCA discovered this without anyone naming sectors. The factors fell out of the eigendecomposition.
Slide 18 (interpret PC3 — growth vs yield): ~1 min. Top loaders: Nvidia, Amazon, Meta, Adobe, Alphabet — big-tech growth. Bottom: utilities (DUK, SO), staples (KO), high-dividend telecom (VZ) — yield/income. By PC5 or PC6 the loadings stop telling crisp economic stories.
Slide 19 (DISCUSSION: did PCA work?): 3 min think-pair-share (1 min think + 2 min pair). Set up the central methodological lesson.
Target answer: no. Beautifully named factors are appealing — they pass the human-narrative test — but they’re not validation. The factors named themselves because we already know the universe. We need a method-agnostic test of whether the components carry useful information.
The chapter’s answer (block 3): use the components on a downstream task and evaluate on data we never used to fit them. Interpretability is not validation.
If a student says “yes, the named factors prove it” — that’s exactly the position the chapter argues against. Hold and let block 3 unsettle it.
Slide 20 (the math — variational form): ~90 sec. Among all \(k\)-dim subspaces of \(\mathbb{R}^p\), PCA picks the one closest to the data. By Pythagoras (after centering): minimizing residual = maximizing projected variance.
Continuity with Pearson: \(k=1\) is Pearson’s line; \(k=2\) is a plane; for our stocks, \(k=3\) is a 3D subspace inside \(\mathbb{R}^{95}\).
Slide 21 (the math — SVD): ~90 sec. The variational form said what; SVD says how. Every \(n \times p\) matrix factors as \(X = USV^T\). First \(k\) columns of \(V\) are PC1, …, PC\(k\). Variance of PC\(_i\) = \(\sigma_i^2 / (n-1)\).
Bottom table is a cheat sheet for students flipping between sklearn docs, finance literature, and our notation. Three communities, three names for the same objects. Don’t dwell — point and move on.
Slide 22 (verify): ~1 min. Five lines of code: SVD components match sklearn’s .components_ up to a sign per PC. Differences at \(10^{-15}\) to \(10^{-17}\) are floating-point noise. The two routines compute the same object, packaged differently.
Worthwhile habit: when a textbook says “X is just Y”, verify on real data.







The reason PCA is in this course: it solves a concrete portfolio problem when the standard formula breaks (\(p > n\)). The walk-forward backtest is the validation that delivers on block 2’s setup — held-out portfolio variance is the proof, not the named factors. End with the Novembre cautionary tale: a beautiful PCA figure isn’t always discovery.
Slide 23 (dark transition): “the payoff: portfolios in the \(p > n\) regime.”
Slide 24 (why minimize variance — shouldn’t we maximize return?): ~90 sec. Address the obvious student objection up front. High variance kills long-run wealth.
Walk through the 50%/50% example: $1 → $0.50 → $0.75. Down 25%, not flat. Compounding penalizes losses more than gains. General statement: \(E[\log(1+r)] \approx \mu - \sigma^2/2\) — variance subtracts directly from compound growth (“volatility drag”).
Practical: low-volatility ETFs (USMV, SPLV) are billion-dollar funds. Pension funds and risk-parity strategies are explicitly variance-minimizing. So minimum-variance isn’t a quirky academic objective — it’s a major chunk of how real money invests.
The parenthetical at the bottom acknowledges: full Markowitz trades off return AND variance. We focus on variance because PCA helps estimate covariance.
Slide 25 (Markowitz — minimum-variance portfolio): ~90 sec. Two-line problem: - Variance of the portfolio’s return is \(w^T \Sigma w\) - Constraint: weights sum to 1 - Solution: \(w \propto \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}\)
Read the inverse-row-sum interpretation: low-vol stocks and natural hedges get larger weights; volatile and correlated stocks get smaller. So we need \(\Sigma^{-1}\). Where do we get \(\Sigma\)? From a recent window of historical returns.
Slide 26 (the failure mode — \(p > n\)): ~1 min. The crisis. 95 stocks, 60 days. The 95×95 sample covariance from 60 observations has rank at most 59 — singular, inverse doesn’t exist.
Adding a small ridge makes it invertible but unstable: weights enormous, change wildly with one-day window shifts. Useless. The math we were taught for \(\Sigma^{-1}\) assumed \(n \gg p\). In high-dim finance, that’s rarely true.
Slide 27 (PCA gives us \(\Sigma\) — factor model): ~90 sec. Instead of estimating the full \(95 \times 95\) from 60 days, constrain it: \(k\) pervasive factors + idiosyncratic noise.
Factor part \(V_k \Lambda_k V_k^T\) captures systematic risk. Diagonal \(D\) accounts for stock-specific risk and makes the estimate positive definite (so the inverse exists).
Catch: we have to pick \(k\). Scree elbow says \(k=1\). CV will say \(k=3\).
Slide 28 (the four estimators): ~90 sec. Equal-weight (baseline), ridge sample (invertibility hack), random projection (sanity check — does PCA’s variance alignment matter?), PCA factor (the contender).
Walk-forward evaluation: train on past 60 days, hold for next 21, slide. Time-respecting CV. Random 5-fold would leak future regimes into the past.
Slide 29 (the result — variance vs \(k\)): ~2 min. Blue (PCA): drops sharply 1→3, flat 3→10, rises past 10. Same bias-variance trade-off as in regression, but for covariance estimation. Gray (random projection): above PCA throughout — variance-aligned directions matter. Red (ridge): worst. Green (equal-weight): beats ridge but lags PCA.
CV picks \(k=3\). Three factors capture the systematic structure; more starts adding noise.
Slide 30 (the result — cumulative variance over time): ~90 sec. PCA factor portfolio sits lowest the entire test period. COVID jump in early 2020: every line shoots up, but PCA’s jump is the smallest of the four. The market’s structure changed fast in March 2020, and a factor-model estimate adapted faster than the alternatives.
Slide 31 (interpretability is not validation): ~1 min. The chapter’s central methodological lesson, and the answer to slide 19’s discussion. The named factors looked impressive, but a named factor is just a story. The proof comes from a downstream task evaluated on data we never used to fit the components.
Eval-by-downstream-task: same idea as cross-validation in regression.
Slide 32 (cautionary tale — genes mirror geography): ~2 min. Novembre et al. ran PCA on ~197K SNPs across 1,387 Europeans, gave the algorithm zero geographic info, and the first two principal components reproduced the map of Europe. PC1 lined up with latitude, PC2 with longitude.
The temptation: read this as “PCA discovered hidden European population structure.” The same research community published the math showing otherwise: Novembre & Stephens (2008, Nat. Gen.) proved that PCA on any spatial data with distance-decaying similarity will produce gradient/sinusoidal patterns generically — no historical demographic event required.
So the famous figure was always going to come out — the math made it inevitable given the sampling. The structure was built into the input geography, not discovered as European prehistory.
The lesson — and a parallel to today’s named-factor discussion: a PCA figure that reproduces something obvious about the data (a map, a known taxonomy) is not by itself evidence that PCA discovered hidden structure. Same lesson next lecture, on clustering. Genes mirror geography → PredPol mirrors policing → MBTI mirrors cutpoints. Three fields, same artifact.
Slide 33 (DISCUSSION: PCA or pick five?): 3 min think-pair-share (1 min think + 2 min pair).
Target answers: - Lasso is right when the truth is sparse — only a handful of features matter, the rest are noise. Example: predict next-quarter earnings from one of 200 specific accounting ratios. You want a short list. - PCA is right when many features carry useful information and you want a low-dim summary that pools them. Example: today’s covariance matrix where every stock is partly driven by a common market factor. A sparse subset would miss the market hitting all of them. - Third option — sparse PCA — splits the difference: PCs in which most loadings are zero.
Deeper point: PCA and Lasso answer different questions. Lasso says “which features matter?” PCA says “what’s a low-dim summary?”





Slide 34 (demo): 30 sec. Notebook link. Three knobs to turn: standardization on/off, vary \(k\), swap the time window. HW 4 has students do exactly this on Airbnb data.
Slide 35 (summary): ~1 min. Six takeaways in lecture order. Last bullet is the headline: pretty loadings tell a story, but the story isn’t the test.
Slide 36 (next: clustering): ~45 sec. Forward pointer to lec15. PCA today gives continuous PC scores in a low-dim subspace. K-means Monday gives a discrete cluster label. Same compress-into-\(k\) template, different geometry. A natural starting move on Monday: cluster the PC scores from today’s analysis.
The “your features pick your structure” lesson reappears: the Novembre cautionary tale today, the PredPol cautionary tale Monday — same lesson on adjacent methods.
Slide 37 (feedback): Standard feedback slide.







Block
Slides
Duration




Opening
1–5
~3 min


Block 1 (picture + standardization)
6–13
~22 min


Block 2 (components + math)
14–22
~22 min


Block 3 (payoff + cautionary tale)
23–33
~28 min


Closing
34–37
~5 min


Total

~80 min



Buffer: ~2 min for transitions and overrun on discussions.



“Why log returns instead of simple returns?” — Log returns add cleanly across time (chapter line at first use). For small daily moves they’re nearly indistinguishable from simple percent returns. Conventional in finance.
“Why does PCA need standardization but k-means tomorrow?” — Same reason: both use Euclidean distance / variance, both let the largest-magnitude feature dominate without standardization. Same fix (z-score). Lec15 will hit this again.
“How do we interpret negative PC2 loadings?” — A PC’s sign is arbitrary (we could flip PC2 → -PC2 and the math is identical). What matters is the contrast — which stocks land on opposite sides. We chose the sign convention so financials are positive and staples are negative.
“Why is PC1’s variance share only ~40%, not higher?” — Because we standardized. Before standardization, TSLA’s huge raw variance dominated, inflating the first PC’s share. After standardization, every stock contributes roughly equally to total variance, and the market factor only captures the correlated part — about 40% on this universe.
“What if I want both factors AND a return objective?” — That’s the full Markowitz mean-variance frontier. We focus on the variance-only problem because covariance estimation is where PCA bites. Once you have a stable \(\hat\Sigma\), plugging into the full mean-variance problem is straightforward (and you need an expected-return estimate, which is its own hard problem).
“How does this scale to 5,000 stocks?” — Same recipe. SVD scales to large matrices; randomized SVD makes very large problems tractable. Real risk models in industry (Barra, Axioma) are essentially fancier factor models with thousands of assets and dozens of factors — still PCA’s variance-aligned-directions idea.

The parenthetical at the bottom acknowledges: full Markowitz trades off return AND variance. We focus on variance because PCA helps estimate covariance.
Slide 25 (Markowitz — minimum-variance portfolio): ~90 sec. Two-line problem: - Variance of the portfolio’s return is \(w^T \Sigma w\) - Constraint: weights sum to 1 - Solution: \(w \propto \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{1}\)
Read the inverse-row-sum interpretation: low-vol stocks and natural hedges get larger weights; volatile and correlated stocks get smaller. So we need \(\Sigma^{-1}\). Where do we get \(\Sigma\)? From a recent window of historical returns.
Slide 26 (the failure mode — \(p > n\)): ~1 min. The crisis. 95 stocks, 60 days. The 95×95 sample covariance from 60 observations has rank at most 59 — singular, inverse doesn’t exist.
Adding a small ridge makes it invertible but unstable: weights enormous, change wildly with one-day window shifts. Useless. The math we were taught for \(\Sigma^{-1}\) assumed \(n \gg p\). In high-dim finance, that’s rarely true.
Slide 27 (PCA gives us \(\Sigma\) — factor model): ~90 sec. Instead of estimating the full \(95 \times 95\) from 60 days, constrain it: \(k\) pervasive factors + idiosyncratic noise.
Factor part \(V_k \Lambda_k V_k^T\) captures systematic risk. Diagonal \(D\) accounts for stock-specific risk and makes the estimate positive definite (so the inverse exists).
Catch: we have to pick \(k\). Scree elbow says \(k=1\). CV will say \(k=3\).
Slide 28 (the four estimators): ~90 sec. Equal-weight (baseline), ridge sample (invertibility hack), random projection (sanity check — does PCA’s variance alignment matter?), PCA factor (the contender).
Walk-forward evaluation: train on past 60 days, hold for next 21, slide. Time-respecting CV. Random 5-fold would leak future regimes into the past.
Slide 29 (the result — variance vs \(k\)): ~2 min. Blue (PCA): drops sharply 1→3, flat 3→10, rises past 10. Same bias-variance trade-off as in regression, but for covariance estimation. Gray (random projection): above PCA throughout — variance-aligned directions matter. Red (ridge): worst. Green (equal-weight): beats ridge but lags PCA.
CV picks \(k=3\). Three factors capture the systematic structure; more starts adding noise.
Slide 30 (the result — cumulative variance over time): ~90 sec. PCA factor portfolio sits lowest the entire test period. COVID jump in early 2020: every line shoots up, but PCA’s jump is the smallest of the four. The market’s structure changed fast in March 2020, and a factor-model estimate adapted faster than the alternatives.
Slide 31 (interpretability is not validation): ~1 min. The chapter’s central methodological lesson, and the answer to slide 19’s discussion. The named factors looked impressive, but a named factor is just a story. The proof comes from a downstream task evaluated on data we never used to fit the components.
Eval-by-downstream-task: same idea as cross-validation in regression.
Slide 32 (cautionary tale — genes mirror geography): ~2 min. Novembre et al. ran PCA on ~197K SNPs across 1,387 Europeans, gave the algorithm zero geographic info, and the first two principal components reproduced the map of Europe. PC1 lined up with latitude, PC2 with longitude.
The temptation: read this as “PCA discovered hidden European population structure.” The same research community published the math showing otherwise: Novembre & Stephens (2008, Nat. Gen.) proved that PCA on any spatial data with distance-decaying similarity will produce gradient/sinusoidal patterns generically — no historical demographic event required.
So the famous figure was always going to come out — the math made it inevitable given the sampling. The structure was built into the input geography, not discovered as European prehistory.
The lesson — and a parallel to today’s named-factor discussion: a PCA figure that reproduces something obvious about the data (a map, a known taxonomy) is not by itself evidence that PCA discovered hidden structure. Same lesson next lecture, on clustering. Genes mirror geography → PredPol mirrors policing → MBTI mirrors cutpoints. Three fields, same artifact.
Slide 33 (DISCUSSION: PCA or pick five?): 3 min think-pair-share (1 min think + 2 min pair).
Target answers: - Lasso is right when the truth is sparse — only a handful of features matter, the rest are noise. Example: predict next-quarter earnings from one of 200 specific accounting ratios. You want a short list. - PCA is right when many features carry useful information and you want a low-dim summary that pools them. Example: today’s covariance matrix where every stock is partly driven by a common market factor. A sparse subset would miss the market hitting all of them. - Third option — sparse PCA — splits the difference: PCs in which most loadings are zero.
Deeper point: PCA and Lasso answer different questions. Lasso says “which features matter?” PCA says “what’s a low-dim summary?”

title: Lecture 14: PCA — Dimensionality Reduction — Facilitation Script
text:
Slide 34 (demo): 30 sec. Notebook link. Three knobs to turn: standardization on/off, vary \(k\), swap the time window. HW 4 has students do exactly this on Airbnb data.
Slide 35 (summary): ~1 min. Six takeaways in lecture order. Last bullet is the headline: pretty loadings tell a story, but the story isn’t the test.
Slide 36 (next: clustering): ~45 sec. Forward pointer to lec15. PCA today gives continuous PC scores in a low-dim subspace. K-means Monday gives a discrete cluster label. Same compress-into-\(k\) template, different geometry. A natural starting move on Monday: cluster the PC scores from today’s analysis.
The “your features pick your structure” lesson reappears: the Novembre cautionary tale today, the PredPol cautionary tale Monday — same lesson on adjacent methods.
Slide 37 (feedback): Standard feedback slide.

title: Lecture 14: PCA — Dimensionality Reduction — Facilitation Script
text:
“Why log returns instead of simple returns?” — Log returns add cleanly across time (chapter line at first use). For small daily moves they’re nearly indistinguishable from simple percent returns. Conventional in finance.
“Why does PCA need standardization but k-means tomorrow?” — Same reason: both use Euclidean distance / variance, both let the largest-magnitude feature dominate without standardization. Same fix (z-score). Lec15 will hit this again.
“How do we interpret negative PC2 loadings?” — A PC’s sign is arbitrary (we could flip PC2 → -PC2 and the math is identical). What matters is the contrast — which stocks land on opposite sides. We chose the sign convention so financials are positive and staples are negative.
“Why is PC1’s variance share only ~40%, not higher?” — Because we standardized. Before standardization, TSLA’s huge raw variance dominated, inflating the first PC’s share. After standardization, every stock contributes roughly equally to total variance, and the market factor only captures the correlated part — about 40% on this universe.
“What if I want both factors AND a return objective?” — That’s the full Markowitz mean-variance frontier. We focus on the variance-only problem because covariance estimation is where PCA bites. Once you have a stable \(\hat\Sigma\), plugging into the full mean-variance problem is straightforward (and you need an expected-return estimate, which is its own hard problem).
“How does this scale to 5,000 stocks?” — Same recipe. SVD scales to large matrices; randomized SVD makes very large problems tractable. Real risk models in industry (Barra, Axioma) are essentially fancier factor models with thousands of assets and dozens of factors — still PCA’s variance-aligned-directions idea.

title: Lecture 7: Classification — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 75 minutes Primary dataset: Framingham Heart Study (CHD, 15% base rate) Hook: 85% accuracy model that catches zero patients Structure: Opening (5 min) → Block 1: Mechanics (~20 min) → Block 2: Accuracy trap (~15 min + 5 min poll) → Block 3: Thresholds (~10 min + 5 min poll) → Block 4: Subgroups + calibration (~10 min + 5 min poll) → Closing (2 min) → Buffer



Slides 1–2 (dark hook + readmissions setup). Open with the drama: the model was 85% accurate and caught zero. Pause. Then the readmissions setup — base rate is 15%, null-model baseline looks like a good model. Key: this is not a toy problem. CMS penalties are real; hospitals actually deploy models like this.
Slide 3 (agenda): Four blocks. Mechanics → accuracy trap → thresholds → what aggregates hide.






We need a classifier. Naive OLS fails because predictions leave [0, 1]. Sigmoid trick → logistic regression. Fit with gradient descent on a convex loss.
Slide 4 (binary outcome): Introduce Framingham. 4,240 patients, 10-year followup. Outcome is 0/1. Stake the vocabulary: CHD = 1 = positive class; no CHD = 0 = negative class. Positive = detected, not desirable — the positive CHD test is bad news. This matters when we talk sensitivity/specificity later.
Slide 5 (linreg fails): Show the scatter. Predictions below 0 and above 1. “You can’t use OLS because nothing constrains predictions to [0, 1].”
Slide 6 (sigmoid): “Rather than constrain OLS, transform what we model.” Push z through σ. Show the S-shape. z = 0 maps to p = 0.5.
Slide 7 (log-odds table): This is the same idea from a different angle. Walk the rows. Log-odds range from −∞ to +∞ — exactly what a linear model can output. Logistic regression = linear model of log-odds.
Slide 8 (Framingham age fit): “We see the lower portion of the S because the base rate is low.” Age alone doesn’t get anyone above 30% predicted risk.
Slide 9 (logistic loss formula): No closed form. Pick a loss. Emphasize the confident wrong penalty.
Slide 10 (loss comparison): Visual — squared stays bounded, logistic explodes. This is the asymmetry that forces the model to avoid overconfidence on hard cases.
Slide 11 (gradient descent): Hiking metaphor. ∇L = uphill, step the opposite way. η = step size. Convex = single bowl (assuming data isn’t separable — don’t belabor, just note).
Slide 12 (GD convergence): Loss drops fast, settles. This is 50 iterations on a 2-feature model; sklearn uses a fancier variant (L-BFGS).







Fit the model, compute accuracy, celebrate → reveal the baseline is identical → unmask accuracy as misleading under class imbalance → introduce confusion matrix, precision, recall.
Slide 13 (dark): Block break.
Slide 14 (fit + test accuracy): 85.5%. Hold it in mind.
Slide 15 (baseline reveal): “Always predict no CHD” scores 84.8%. Our model improves by 0.7 points. This is the lecture’s most important beat — land it slowly.
Slide 16 (accuracy trap callout): General principle. Accuracy is population-averaged. With imbalance, the common class dominates.
Slide 17 (confusion matrix): Walk through the four cells. TN dominates. Bottom row is where we care about — only 17 of 167 CHD cases caught. “Accuracy hid this because there are so many true negatives.”
Slide 18 (precision vs recall definitions): Two metrics, two questions. Precision = “trust the flag?” Recall = “of all the sick, how many did we find?”
Slide 19 (our P and R): 63% precision, 8% recall. A disaster for a screening tool.
Slide 20 (discussion poll): FP vs FN costs — $500 vs tens of thousands. Students commit. Then flip: spam filter reverses the costs. Key lesson: the threshold is a cost-benefit call, not a statistical one. 2 minutes max. Don’t resolve — transition to Block 3.







Sweep thresholds. ROC for ranking; PR for tradeoff; actual metrics at 5 thresholds to make it concrete.
Slide 21 (dark): Block break.
Slide 22 (ROC): Perfect model hugs top-left. Ours is 0.75 AUC. Concordance probability — “pick one CHD, one non-CHD, model ranks correctly 75% of the time.” Clean, threshold-free.
Slide 23 (PR curve): Same information, different view. No dominant point — you must choose.
Slide 24 (threshold effect bars): Concrete numbers at 5 thresholds. Low threshold → catch more, trust each flag less.







Three ways a “good” aggregate metric can mask disaster: base rates, subgroups, calibration.
Slide 25 (dark): Block break.
Slide 26 (base rate callout): Set up the 99% claim. Pause. Ask students to commit to an answer before the reveal.
Slide 27 (10,000 patient visualization): Walk through the arithmetic. 50 cancer cases + 100 false positives = ~33% posterior. “The ‘99% accuracy’ was true; the patient’s P(cancer | positive) is 33%.” Base rate neglect. This is why mass screening programs are controversial.
Slide 28 (subgroup discussion poll): Let students predict which group the model fails for. Most will say “young patients” because age is the dominant feature. Good — set up the reveal. 2 minutes max.
Slide 29 (subgroup AUC): Reveal. Under-40 AUC ≈ 0.36. Below 0.5 = worse than random. Inverting the ranking would do better. Caveat: only ~6 CHD cases in 191 patients, so the estimate is noisy — but the direction is real. The model learned “older = sicker” and nothing else.
Slide 30 (boxplot): Same failure in a different view. Young: everything bunched at zero. Old: red above blue. The model is essentially blind to young-patient risk.
Slide 31 (calibration plot): New question. Do probabilities mean what they say? Decile bins → observed vs predicted. Our model tracks the diagonal reasonably well. It’s well-calibrated, despite the ranking failures above. These are different properties.
Slide 32 (AUC vs calibration): Two properties. AUC = ranking, invariant to monotone rescaling. Calibration = absolute values. Which matters depends on how you use the output.





Slide 33 (dark): Closing loop.
Slide 34 (back to readmissions): Return to the opening hook. Now students know what to ask: baseline, precision/recall, ROC, subgroups, calibration. The operating-point choice is a cost-benefit decision, not a statistical one.
Slide 35 (takeaways): Six bullets. Reveal incrementally.
Slide 36 (next time): Forward pointers. Act 2 opens with bootstrap, which gives CIs on today’s metrics. Ch 12 does formal inference on logistic coefficients. Ch 13 adds trees.




If running short (< 60 min available): - Cut the GD convergence slide (slide 12); just mention it. - Skip the PR curve (slide 23); threshold bars (slide 24) cover the same tradeoff. - Shorten subgroup block to just the AUC bar chart (slide 29); skip boxplot.
If running long (> 80 min): - Add a worked confusion matrix exercise after slide 17: “if the threshold is 0.3, fill in the cells.” - Expand the base rate neglect exercise: have students compute for 0.1% prevalence, then 1%, then 10%, and graph P(cancer | positive) vs prevalence.





Slide 20 — FP vs FN tradeoff. Commit cards or PollEv. 2 minutes. Goal: students internalize that the tradeoff is context-dependent.
Slide 28 — which subgroup fails? Think-pair. 2 minutes. Goal: students anticipate the reveal, which makes slide 29 land harder.






Readmissions hook (opening + closing): ties statistics to a real medical operations problem. CMS HRRP penalties are real — up to 3% of Medicare reimbursements.
Framingham (throughout): 1948 start, 3 generations followed. Named after Framingham, Massachusetts. Identified cholesterol, BP, and smoking as cardiovascular risk factors.
99% accurate cancer test (base rate): a stand-in for mammography critiques — high sensitivity and specificity, low prevalence, and low positive predictive value. Real debate: 2009 USPSTF recommendation against annual mammography under 50 was driven by exactly this math.
