title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 80 minutes Primary dataset: NYC Airbnb (5-listing toy example + 28,761 full dataset) Structure: Opening (5 min) → Block 1: Vectors (15 min + 5 min) → Block 2: Linear models + regression (15 min + 5 min) → Block 3: Multiple regression + R² (15 min + 5 min) → Closing (5 min) → Buffer (5 min)



Hook (slide 2, dark): “How much is a bathroom worth?” — an Airbnb host adding a second bathroom. We’ll answer this by the end of the lecture.
Agenda (slide 3): vectors, linear combinations, column space, regression, R². Jump straight into Block 1.






Data points are vectors. Once we see them as vectors, we can measure distance (norm), similarity (inner product), and direction (cosine similarity). This language will let us understand what a linear model does.



Slide 9 (two views): Show the toy table. Two views: row = point, column = vector. Emphasize this is the same data seen two ways.
Slide 10 (scatter): Point at “Listings 1 & 5” — same features, same point. Listing 3 is farthest away.
Slide 11 (difference vector): Code on slide — no context switch needed. The difference vector says how they differ.
Slide 12 (norm): Definition box. “Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.” Show the code: np.linalg.norm(diff) = 2.24.
Slide 13 (inner product): Definition box. Positive = same direction, negative = opposite, zero = perpendicular. Show code.
Slide 14 (cosine similarity): Formula, then code. “0.98 means very similar proportions.”




Format: Poll + predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “beds vs bedrooms — high or low cosine? beds vs reviews — high or low?”
If stuck: “If you know bedrooms, can you guess beds? What about reviews?”
Key insight: beds/bedrooms = 0.65 (redundant), beds/reviews = 0.10 (independent). Independent features are more valuable.
Reveal: Show slide 16 with the answer table.








A linear model is a weighted sum of feature columns. The set of all possible predictions = column space. Regression finds the closest point in that space to the true prices.
Slide 17 (dark): “what can your model reach?”
Slide 18 (linear combination code): Show the computation — 100×bedrooms + 50×bathrooms. Code + output on the slide. “This is ALL a linear model does.”
Slide 19 (definition): Linear combination definition.
Slide 20 (span image): The lattice of all integer-weight combinations. “With real weights, you can reach every point in the plane.”




Format: Think-pair-share (3 min)
Setup: “Can you reach every point in R²? What if v₁ and v₂ point the same direction?”
If stuck: “What shape do you get from multiples of a single vector?”
Key insight: Non-parallel = span all of R². Parallel = only a line. Redundant features don’t help.

Slide 22 (3D projection): Crucial image. “Two vectors in R³ span a plane. The target is off the plane — the closest point is the projection.” Point out the right-angle mark.
Slides 23-24 (column space): Definition, then code. X @ beta computes everything at once.
Slide 25 (dark): “regression = projection”
Slides 26-28 (real data): Load 28,761 listings. Show scatter + regression line. ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms.
Slide 29 (inline Q/A): “What does the intercept mean?” Reveal: $71 for a studio.
Slide 30 (why squared?): Brief — two reasons (projection geometry, conditional mean). “We could choose differently — and we will, in Ch 13.”







Adding features expands the column space. Multiple regression lets us “hold constant.” The normal equations explain why. R² measures fit quality. Residual plots reveal what the model misses.
Slides 31-32 (regression to the mean): Galton story. “Since |r| < 1, predictions are pulled toward the mean.” Connect to the name “regression.”




Format: Predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “Player scores 45 (average: 25). Predict next game?”
If stuck: “Is the 45 more likely a fluke or the new normal?”
Key insight: Below 45, above 25. Coaches who overreact to extreme games are ignoring regression to the mean.

Slides 34-35 (multiple regression): Code + coefficient table. “Each bathroom ~$22, private room −$90.”
Slide 36 (answer!): “~$22/night for that second bathroom.” R² jumped from 0.16 to 0.45.




Format: Poll (2 min)
Setup: “Positive, negative, or zero?”
Key insight: Negative. Cheaper listings get booked more → more reviews. Association ≠ causation.

Slides 38-39 (orthogonality): Column space cartoon + normal equations. “The residual has zero dot product with every feature.”
Slide 40 (verify in code): Show the orthogonality check — both dot products ≈ 0.
Slide 41 (R² formula): Pythagorean decomposition. Total = explained + residual.




Format: Think-pair-share (4 min)
Setup: “Think about the geometry: adding a column makes the column space…”
If stuck: “Can a projection onto a bigger space be farther from the target?”
Key insight: Training R² always increases. Test R² can decrease (overfitting, Ch 7).

Slide 42 (residual plots): Fan shape = heteroscedasticity. “The model misses something.”





Slide 44 (key takeaways): Walk through all six. The chain: vectors → linear combinations → column space → projection → orthogonality → R².
Slide 45 (what we can’t answer yet): Three open questions → forward pointers to Ch 5, 7, 12.
Slide 46 (logistics): Read Ch 4, HW 1 due Friday, Quiz 2 next Wednesday.
Slide 47 (feedback): PollEv link.

Francis Galton studied 928 father-son pairs in the 1880s. Sons of tall fathers were tall but less extreme; sons of short fathers were short but less extreme. He called it “regression toward mediocrity” and the method he used to quantify it became “regression.” (Salsburg, The Lady Tasting Tea, Ch. 4.)



The projection ŷ won’t equal y exactly. The model is wrong. But it’s the best approximation within the column space. Use this framing when introducing the column space.



From the Cornell version: scaffold computational complexity with inline polls. “How many flops to compute u·v?” → “How many for Xw?” Each builds on the previous. Good for math-heavy sections if you need to fill time.

Slide 11 (difference vector): Code on slide — no context switch needed. The difference vector says how they differ.
Slide 12 (norm): Definition box. “Generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.” Show the code: np.linalg.norm(diff) = 2.24.
Slide 13 (inner product): Definition box. Positive = same direction, negative = opposite, zero = perpendicular. Show code.
Slide 14 (cosine similarity): Formula, then code. “0.98 means very similar proportions.”




Format: Poll + predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “beds vs bedrooms — high or low cosine? beds vs reviews — high or low?”
If stuck: “If you know bedrooms, can you guess beds? What about reviews?”
Key insight: beds/bedrooms = 0.65 (redundant), beds/reviews = 0.10 (independent). Independent features are more valuable.
Reveal: Show slide 16 with the answer table.

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
A linear model is a weighted sum of feature columns. The set of all possible predictions = column space. Regression finds the closest point in that space to the true prices.
Slide 17 (dark): “what can your model reach?”
Slide 18 (linear combination code): Show the computation — 100×bedrooms + 50×bathrooms. Code + output on the slide. “This is ALL a linear model does.”
Slide 19 (definition): Linear combination definition.
Slide 20 (span image): The lattice of all integer-weight combinations. “With real weights, you can reach every point in the plane.”




Format: Think-pair-share (3 min)
Setup: “Can you reach every point in R²? What if v₁ and v₂ point the same direction?”
If stuck: “What shape do you get from multiples of a single vector?”
Key insight: Non-parallel = span all of R². Parallel = only a line. Redundant features don’t help.

Slide 22 (3D projection): Crucial image. “Two vectors in R³ span a plane. The target is off the plane — the closest point is the projection.” Point out the right-angle mark.
Slides 23-24 (column space): Definition, then code. X @ beta computes everything at once.
Slide 25 (dark): “regression = projection”
Slides 26-28 (real data): Load 28,761 listings. Show scatter + regression line. ŷ = 71 + 49 × bedrooms.
Slide 29 (inline Q/A): “What does the intercept mean?” Reveal: $71 for a studio.
Slide 30 (why squared?): Brief — two reasons (projection geometry, conditional mean). “We could choose differently — and we will, in Ch 13.”

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Adding features expands the column space. Multiple regression lets us “hold constant.” The normal equations explain why. R² measures fit quality. Residual plots reveal what the model misses.
Slides 31-32 (regression to the mean): Galton story. “Since |r| < 1, predictions are pulled toward the mean.” Connect to the name “regression.”




Format: Predict-then-reveal (3 min)
Setup: “Player scores 45 (average: 25). Predict next game?”
If stuck: “Is the 45 more likely a fluke or the new normal?”
Key insight: Below 45, above 25. Coaches who overreact to extreme games are ignoring regression to the mean.

Slides 34-35 (multiple regression): Code + coefficient table. “Each bathroom ~$22, private room −$90.”
Slide 36 (answer!): “~$22/night for that second bathroom.” R² jumped from 0.16 to 0.45.




Format: Poll (2 min)
Setup: “Positive, negative, or zero?”
Key insight: Negative. Cheaper listings get booked more → more reviews. Association ≠ causation.

Slides 38-39 (orthogonality): Column space cartoon + normal equations. “The residual has zero dot product with every feature.”
Slide 40 (verify in code): Show the orthogonality check — both dot products ≈ 0.
Slide 41 (R² formula): Pythagorean decomposition. Total = explained + residual.




Format: Think-pair-share (4 min)
Setup: “Think about the geometry: adding a column makes the column space…”
If stuck: “Can a projection onto a bigger space be farther from the target?”
Key insight: Training R² always increases. Test R² can decrease (overfitting, Ch 7).

Slide 42 (residual plots): Fan shape = heteroscedasticity. “The model misses something.”

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Slide 44 (key takeaways): Walk through all six. The chain: vectors → linear combinations → column space → projection → orthogonality → R².
Slide 45 (what we can’t answer yet): Three open questions → forward pointers to Ch 5, 7, 12.
Slide 46 (logistics): Read Ch 4, HW 1 due Friday, Quiz 2 next Wednesday.
Slide 47 (feedback): PollEv link.

title: Lecture 4: Linear Models — Facilitation Script
text:
Francis Galton studied 928 father-son pairs in the 1880s. Sons of tall fathers were tall but less extreme; sons of short fathers were short but less extreme. He called it “regression toward mediocrity” and the method he used to quantify it became “regression.” (Salsburg, The Lady Tasting Tea, Ch. 4.)



The projection ŷ won’t equal y exactly. The model is wrong. But it’s the best approximation within the column space. Use this framing when introducing the column space.



From the Cornell version: scaffold computational complexity with inline polls. “How many flops to compute u·v?” → “How many for Xw?” Each builds on the previous. Good for math-heavy sections if you need to fill time.

title: Lecture 12: Regression Inference + Diagnostics — Facilitation Script
text:
Duration: 80 minutes Total slides: 41 (slide 1 = auto title from YAML) Primary datasets: Airbnb NYC listings (14,689 entire-apt listings under $500/night) and NBA player-game data (72,156 player-games, REST_DAYS ≤ 7) Hook: parallel decision problems — Airbnb host considering a $50K bathroom renovation; NBA front office considering schedule restructuring around rest. Same regression machinery, very different verdicts. Structure: Opening (3 min, slides 1–4) → Block 1: Airbnb regression (~22 min, slides 5–15) → Block 2: NBA cautionary tale (~22 min, slides 16–26) → Block 3: diagnostics (~22 min, slides 27–35) → Logistic coda (~7 min, slides 36–38) → Closing (4 min, slides 39–41) → Buffer (~0 min)



Slide 1 (title): 30 seconds. Date, name.
Slide 2 (logistics): START RECORDING. 30 seconds. HW 3 due Friday — uses regression inference directly. Project proposals were submitted last week; feedback returned next week.
Slide 3 (the brief): Frame the lecture as two parallel decision problems. Your friend is an Airbnb host considering a $50K renovation; another friend at an NBA front office wants to restructure around rest. Same toolkit will give very different answers. Don’t tease the punchlines — the bathroom CI lands in block 1, the rest effect-size in block 2.
Slide 4 (today): Four beats: Airbnb regression (read the table), NBA cautionary tale, diagnostics, logistic coda. Each block uses the same template established in block 1.






Establish the canonical regression-table reading template. Walk through the bathrooms coefficient end to end (sign, SE, t, p, CI). The decision-grade output: bathroom premium $59–$66/night, payback ~3 years.
Slide 5 (dark block marker). “regression for decisions.”
Slide 6 (load + filter): ~1 min. Filter to entire-apt listings under $500 with bathroom/bedroom data — 14,689 listings. The next slide shows what they look like.
Slide 7 (price by bathrooms and borough): ~1 min. Boxplots show the obvious patterns: more bathrooms → higher price; Manhattan > Brooklyn > rest. But the bathrooms picture is confounded by bedrooms (more rooms → more of both). Multivariate regression isolates the bathroom premium.
Slide 8 (DISCUSSION: predict before you fit): 45 sec inline predict. Students write down guesses for (1) bathrooms coefficient and (2) Manhattan-vs-Bronx gap. Most will guess $50–$100 for each; both are in that ballpark.
Slide 9 (the regression table): ~2 min. Display model.summary(). Don’t try to teach all eight elements at once — point at intercept, borough rows (gap relative to Bronx), bathrooms ($62.44), bedrooms ($34). The next slide makes the column structure explicit.
Slide 10 (reading the table): ~3 min. Five columns per row + header (R², F) + footer (Cond. No.). Walk through bathrooms: $62.44 (coef), $1.86 (SE), 33.6 (t), <0.001 (p), [$58.80, $66.09] (CI). R² = 0.40, Cond. No. = 35 (just over the multicollinearity threshold; bathrooms ↔︎ bedrooms expected). F-statistic in the Going-deeper note — not used in this course.
Slide 11 (interpreting the coefficients): ~2 min. Read the signs. Bathroom premium ≈ $62. Bedroom $34. Manhattan +$79 vs Bronx. Brooklyn +$26 vs Bronx. Queens, Staten Island not significantly different from Bronx. The “holding everything else constant” gloss is what students should understand by “controlling for.”
Slide 12 (DISCUSSION: would adding a bedroom raise YOUR price by $34?): 4 minutes. 1 min think + 2 min pair + 1 min debrief. Get students to see that the regression coefficient is an across-listings association, not a personal intervention prediction. Adding a bedroom usually means a bigger apartment — many things change at once. Key insight: an across-listings association is not a counterfactual claim about your unit.
Slide 13 (the conditional null): ~2 min. H₀ is conditional, not unconditional. The same predictor can fail in one model and pass in another. Students saw exactly this in Ch 5 — the bathrooms coefficient was bigger before bedrooms entered. Critical for block 2: the NBA REST_DAYS test is “does rest add anything beyond player quality, opponent strength, home/away?”
Slide 14 (the t-statistic): ~2 min. Gosset callback to Ch 10. When you estimate the SE from data, the ratio has heavier tails — Student’s t. n is large here (~15,000) so t* ≈ 1.96. Multiple-testing aside (Lec 11): a regression with p predictors runs p tests at once; with the p-values seen here (<0.001) Bonferroni doesn’t change conclusions, but for marginal p-values it would.
Slide 15 (CI for bathrooms): ~2 min. Plug in: 62.44 ± 1.96 × 1.86 = [$58.80, $66.09]. Bootstrap on 1,000 resamples agrees ([$58.78, $66.04]). The decision-grade output. Width ~$7 is tight enough to use; lower bound $59 clears the renovation hurdle.
Slide 16 (decision: invest $50K?): ~2 min. $59–$66/night × 256 nights/year (70% occupancy × 365) = ~$15K–$17K/year. Payback ~3 years. Yes, the data support the renovation — with two caveats: (a) association ≠ intervention, and (b) we need to verify the diagnostics (block 3).







Run the same machinery on a question where the data refuse to confirm the prior. REST_DAYS is “significant” at p < 0.001 with n = 72,000, but Cohen’s d ≈ -0.02. Significance ≠ importance. Then back up to Simpson’s paradox: even the small effect is suspect because of confounding.
Slide 17 (dark block marker). “same machinery, different verdict.”
Slide 18 (the NBA question): ~1 min. Setup the confounding sketch: bench players, stars, injured players all have non-random rest patterns. The four-predictor model: REST_DAYS + PLAYER_SEASON_AVG + HOME + OPP_GS_ALLOWED.
Slide 19 (fit, then read): ~2 min. Display model.summary(). REST_DAYS row: -0.15, p < 0.001, CI [-0.20, -0.11]. Don’t reveal the punchline — let students sit with “the data say rest hurts performance, p < 0.001” for a moment.
Slide 20 (but check the size): ~3 min. Cohen’s d definition: coefficient divided by response SD. For REST_DAYS: -0.15 / 7.85 = -0.0195 SD. Essentially zero. Caveat: Cohen’s benchmarks are conventions, not laws. Domain context overrides.
Slide 21 (significance ≠ importance): ~2 min. The pivotal table. NBA REST_DAYS and Airbnb bathrooms both clear every textbook bar (p < 0.001, CI excludes 0). Cohen’s d is 15× larger for bathrooms. Practically meaningful: yes for Airbnb, no for NBA. The aphorism caps the block.
Slide 22 (DISCUSSION: questions to ask the analytics team): 5 minutes. 1 min think + 2 min pair + 2 min debrief. Target answers: (1) how big is the effect, (2) is there confounding, (3) does the model capture all relevant factors, (4) would this hold out of sample. Push for #3 and #4. Key insight: a tight CI does not protect you from any of these — CIs widen with sample noise, not with confounding.
Slide 23 (standardized coefficients): ~1 min. Bar chart with 95% CIs. Player quality dominates; REST_DAYS barely clears zero. The bar that should drive a strategic decision is invisible.
Slide 24 (variable names can deceive): ~2 min. The OPP_GS_ALLOWED warning. We named the variable mean GS allowed by the opponent, so high = porous defense. The positive coefficient is mechanical. Lesson: a coefficient’s sign is only interpretable if you know how the variable was constructed. Real-world failure mode.
Slide 25 (confounding made visible): ~3 min. Simpson’s-paradox-in-regression visualization. Aggregate slope (dashed) is steeper than within-quartile slopes (colored). Adding PLAYER_SEASON_AVG = switching from the dashed line to the colored ones. Strictly, this is the attenuation form of Simpson’s, not a sign-flip.
Slide 26 (association, not causation): ~2 min. Coaches choose when to rest — DNP after a soft loss differs from DNP before a back-to-back. Even with all measured controls, the rest coefficient mixes the rest effect with strategic-scheduling effects. Forward to Ch 18: DAGs.







Same template, sharper purpose. LINE conditions checked via residual plot (L, E), Q-Q plot (N), and — for I — by asking whether rows are clustered. Both models show mild heteroscedasticity and right-skew. Coefficient inference survives via CLT; prediction intervals are dicier. The chapter’s strongest sentence: diagnostics catch statistical, not systematic, problems.
Slide 27 (dark block marker). “is the inference trustworthy?”
Slide 28 (LINE conditions): ~2 min. Definition box. Each letter has a signature. Independence is hardest to check from residuals alone — both datasets cluster (NBA repeats players; Airbnb clusters by host). Clustered SEs are the standard fix; we leave it to homework. Reassure: with n in the tens of thousands, clustering would widen CIs but not flip “significant vs not” calls.
Slide 29 (residual plot — Airbnb): ~2 min. Spread fans out as fitted price grows — textbook funnel. Mild heteroscedasticity (E violation). Standard fix: log-transform price.
Slide 30 (residual plot — NBA): ~2 min. Symmetric around zero in residuals-vs-fitted, but scale-location LOWESS rises 0.2 → 1.0. Same E-violation, different form: low-projection players have less room to deviate; high-projection stars vary more game-to-game.
Slide 31 (Q-Q plot): ~2 min. Both Airbnb and NBA show right-skew (right tail bows above the line). Airbnb: prices have a hard floor at $0 but a long right tail. NBA: biggest games (12-for-15 from three) happen more often than a normal would predict.
Slide 32 (Q-Q gallery): ~1 min. Reference figure. Six distributions paired with their Q-Q plots. Each shape (concave, convex, S, reverse-S, gap) maps to a residual pattern. Don’t dwell — students should just see “right-skew = convex bowing up” matches both our examples.
Slide 33 (PI vs CI for the mean): ~2 min. Two intervals, two questions. Blue band: CI for the mean — narrow. Orange band: prediction interval — wide. Where data is dense (near 1 bathroom), the PI brackets most of the gray scatter — exactly the job of a 95% PI. The red shaded zone marks where the band is extrapolated beyond the data.
Slide 34 (diagnostics catch statistical, not systematic): ~3 min. The chapter’s strongest single sentence. NBA REST_DAYS is the canonical case — tight CI, clean diagnostics, but the coefficient is the wrong answer to a causal question because rest assignment is correlated with unmeasured strategic factors. Confounders bias the point estimate; they don’t show up in the SE. Bridge to Ch 18.
Slide 35 (DISCUSSION: spot the LINE violation): 5 minutes. 30 sec think + 2 min pair + 2 min debrief. Diagnostic exercise: regression of monthly health-insurance claim cost; Q-Q plot is convex with right tail bowing up. N is failing — right-skew, common in cost data because of catastrophic claims. Standard fix: log-transform the response. Coefficients become “fractional change per unit predictor.” Probe: would clustering help? No, that’s I. Would adding predictors help? Possibly L, not N directly. (If running short on time, skip this discussion.)







Same template, just z replaces t. Coefficients exponentiate to odds ratios.
Slide 36 (dark transition). “the same template, with z.”
Slide 37 (logistic regression: same recipe): ~2 min. Two-column comparison: linear vs logistic. t→z, exact-LINE→asymptotic-MLE, coef in original units → e^β = odds ratio, CI exponentiates. Read the table the same way.
Slide 38 (Framingham): ~3 min. Forest plot. Age, smoking, sysBP, glucose all have OR > 1 with CIs excluding 1. BMI’s CI crosses 1 (not distinguishable from no effect, given the other predictors). Walk age concretely: standardized OR ≈ 1.78 per 1 SD (~9 years); 50→60 multiplies CHD odds by ~1.95.





Slide 39 (summary): ~2 min. Five takeaways: Airbnb act-on-it, NBA don’t-act-on-it, same machinery → check effect size not just p-value, diagnostics catch statistical not systematic, logistic = same template with z.
Slide 40 (next: Ch 12.5): ~1 min. Bootstrap CI for AUC, permutation test for classifier vs random, multiple-testing on logistic coefficients. Same template — only the test statistic and the response variable change. Then Ch 13 opens Act 3 with trees and random forests.
Slide 41 (feedback): ~30 sec. QR code + URL.














#
Slide
Format
Duration
Purpose




1
8
Inline predict-then-reveal
45 sec
Predict bathrooms coef + Manhattan-vs-Bronx gap before fit


2
12
Think-pair-share
4 min
Bedroom causal interpretation — across-listings vs intervention


3
22
Think-pair-share
5 min
Questions to ask the analytics team — surface significance/confounding/scope/generalization


4
35
Analyze-this
5 min
Diagnose the LINE violation in a fictional health-insurance regression



Total ~15 min of activity in 80 min — within target band.




In order of preference: 1. Cut slide 35 discussion (analyze-this on health-insurance) — saves 5 min. Block 3 still has the diagnostics walk-through. 2. Compress slide 32 (Q-Q gallery) to a wave-and-mention — saves 1 min. 3. Cut slide 26 (association, not causation) to verbal aside on slide 25 — saves 2 min.
Don’t cut: the conditional null (slide 13), Cohen’s d (slide 20), the analytics-team discussion (slide 22), or the systematic-vs-statistical warning (slide 34) — those are the chapter’s pedagogical spine.





Render: cd slides && quarto render lec12-regression-inference.qmd — clean.
Screenshot: node tools/screenshot-slides.js _site/lec12-regression-inference.html — 41 PNGs in /tmp/slides-review.
All cell-N image references match the book chapter render (cells 5, 11, 15, 17, 19, 20, 21, 22, 28 + fig-qq-gallery).

title: Lecture 12: Regression Inference + Diagnostics — Facilitation Script
text:
Same template, just z replaces t. Coefficients exponentiate to odds ratios.
Slide 36 (dark transition). “the same template, with z.”
Slide 37 (logistic regression: same recipe): ~2 min. Two-column comparison: linear vs logistic. t→z, exact-LINE→asymptotic-MLE, coef in original units → e^β = odds ratio, CI exponentiates. Read the table the same way.
Slide 38 (Framingham): ~3 min. Forest plot. Age, smoking, sysBP, glucose all have OR > 1 with CIs excluding 1. BMI’s CI crosses 1 (not distinguishable from no effect, given the other predictors). Walk age concretely: standardized OR ≈ 1.78 per 1 SD (~9 years); 50→60 multiplies CHD odds by ~1.95.

title: Lecture 12: Regression Inference + Diagnostics — Facilitation Script
text:
Slide 39 (summary): ~2 min. Five takeaways: Airbnb act-on-it, NBA don’t-act-on-it, same machinery → check effect size not just p-value, diagnostics catch statistical not systematic, logistic = same template with z.
Slide 40 (next: Ch 12.5): ~1 min. Bootstrap CI for AUC, permutation test for classifier vs random, multiple-testing on logistic coefficients. Same template — only the test statistic and the response variable change. Then Ch 13 opens Act 3 with trees and random forests.
Slide 41 (feedback): ~30 sec. QR code + URL.

Don’t cut: the conditional null (slide 13), Cohen’s d (slide 20), the analytics-team discussion (slide 22), or the systematic-vs-statistical warning (slide 34) — those are the chapter’s pedagogical spine.

title: Lecture 3: Data Munging — Facilitation Script
text:
Total time: 80 minutes (~75 planned + 5 buffer) Notebook: Colab link Chapter: Book chapter 3 (lec03-munging.qmd)




Open the Colab notebook and clear all outputs
Test that the notebook runs (scorecard.csv loads, field_of_study.csv loads)
Have the slide deck open in presenter view
Check that the feedback QR code link works
Bookmark the Stanford earnings section of the notebook — you’ll want to show it live







Slide: “concept check from last time”
Display the Airbnb question. Give 15 seconds of silent thought. Advance to reveal the question: “what information would you need?”
Students should say: comparable listings, location, neighborhood, season, amenities. Push back if anyone says “just the average”: “the average of what? All SF listings? All 4-bedrooms? All 4-bedrooms within a mile?” This sets up today’s theme: every analytical choice is a decision.



Slide: montage — debt-vs-GDP chart
“2010. Two Harvard economists publish a paper that becomes the foundation for austerity budgets across Europe. Government debt above 90% of GDP means negative growth — that’s the headline. Three years later, a grad student trying to replicate their results for a homework assignment discovers the error.”



Slide: “the spreadsheet”
“Here’s the actual spreadsheet. The formula at the bottom says AVERAGE L30 to L44. But the data goes to row 49. Five countries — Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark — are excluded. The corrected result flips the finding from minus 0.1% to plus 2.2%.”
Let students absorb. Don’t rush this.



Slide: “three data disasters”
Three columns. Read each briefly: “A cell range. A row limit. An auto-conversion. Three different tools, one pattern: a tool made a decision without warning.”



Slide: “every cleaning decision changes the answer”
Pause 3 seconds. Don’t speak.



Slide: “today’s concepts”
Read the five items quickly. “By the end of class, you’ll have a checklist for every new dataset.”



Slide: “recap: what we found last time”
Brief. “Last time: Airbnb data was relatively tidy. NaN for missing values, pandas handled it. But we noticed some dtypes looked wrong. Today: real datasets are rarely so cooperative.”







Slide: “block 1: the many faces of missing data”



Slide: “today’s dataset: College Scorecard”
“The US Department of Education publishes this every year. 7,700 institutions, 122 columns. It has something most datasets don’t: a data dictionary, methodology documentation, public updates. Before you clean a dataset, ask where it came from.”
Advance to reveal the Kaggle contrast. “Contrast: a random CSV on Kaggle with no documentation. You can’t even know if the data was collected or generated by an AI.”



Slide: “data provenance”
Show definition box. Then advance through the checkmarks. “In the AI era, provenance is breaking down — synthetic datasets, undocumented web scrapes, training data contamination.”



Slides: “a column that should be numeric” → “the culprit: PrivacySuppressed”
First slide: “MD_EARN_WNE_P10. Median earnings 10 years after enrollment. Should be a number. dtype: string.” Pause. “Why?”
Advance to reveal. “PrivacySuppressed. A human-readable phrase sitting in a numeric column. 480 schools. One string forces the entire column to be non-numeric.”



Slide: “the missingness zoo”
Walk through the table quickly. “NaN is the easy case — pandas handles it. Everything else requires active detection.” Don’t dwell on each row.



Slide: Demo slide
Switch to the notebook. Show: 1. Load scorecard, check dtypes, note the str columns 2. Find non-numeric values in MD_EARN_WNE_P10 3. Count PrivacySuppressed across all columns (7 columns affected) 4. Bin schools by enrollment size, show suppression rate by size
Don’t run the entire chapter — just enough to establish the pattern.



Slide: discussion — “if you drop those rows…”
“Think for one minute: if you drop the PrivacySuppressed rows, what kind of schools remain? Would the average earnings go up or down?”
After 1 min think + 2 min pair: take 2-3 responses.
If stuck: “Think about which schools have small cohorts — rural? specialized? under-resourced?”
Key insight: Dropping these rows removes small, rural, specialized schools. The remaining sample is biased toward large institutions. Average earnings probably goes up — small schools tend to serve lower-earning populations. This is selection bias.



Slide: bar chart — suppression by size
“Nearly a third of the smallest schools are suppressed. Among 2,000+ students: about 1%. This isn’t random.”



Slide: “three patterns of missingness”
Show definition box. “MCAR: truly random — mold destroys random records. MAR: depends on things we can see — older patients skip surveys, and we have their age. MNAR: depends on the missing value itself — the sickest patients don’t report, and we never observe their outcomes.”
Advance to reveal: “College Scorecard earnings: MNAR. Small cohort means suppressed. Small schools are systematically different.”
Vivid example from the book: “In ambulance data, a missing heart rate often means the EMTs were too busy saving the patient. The missing value is the signal.”







Slide: “block 2: joining datasets”



Slide: “why join?”
“The Scorecard comes in two files. Institution-level: one row per school. Program-level: one row per school-program pair.” Advance to reveal the questions. “To answer richer questions, we need to combine them.”



Slides: toy example → inner join → left join (3 slides)
Show the Students and Majors tables side by side. “Before I show you the result — which students survive an inner join on major_id?” Take quick answers.
Advance to inner join result. “Alice and Bob survive. Carol is gone — no match for 103. History is gone — no match for 104. Neither table warns you.”
Advance to left join. “All three students survive. Carol’s major becomes NaN — a signal that she has no matching major. That signal is lost in an inner join.”



Slide: “one-to-many: row multiplication”
Show left and right tables. “Two rows on the left, three on the right, all with key A.” Pause. “How many rows in the result?”
Advance to reveal. “Six. 2 × 3 = 6. Joins can GROW your data, not just shrink it.”



Slide: “defense: check your key before joining”
Show the assert. “One line of code. An assert that passes is documentation. An assert that fails is an alarm.”



Slide: discussion — “predict: how many rows…”
“The scorecard has 7,703 institutions. Programs has 228,000 rows across 5,500 institutions. How many rows will the inner join produce? More or fewer than 7,703?”
Most students will say “fewer” — they’re thinking of inner joins as filters. The answer is ~216,000 — a massive expansion because each institution matches to many programs.
If stuck: “Each institution matches to many programs. Stanford alone has 180+.”
Key insight: The inner join is one-to-many — it expands, not contracts. Students who predicted “fewer” were thinking about key overlap (correct for one-to-one joins) but missed the one-to-many expansion.



Slide: Demo slide
Switch to notebook. Show: 1. The assert on UNITID uniqueness 2. The inner join: 7,703 → ~216,000 rows 3. The left join: preserves all 7,703 institutions 4. The bar chart comparing row counts
“7,700 rows became 216,000. And neither join warned you.”



Slide: “after every join: check row counts”
Walk through the table. “Inner joins can gain AND lose rows at the same time — one-to-many expansion plus unmatched key dropping. Left joins gain rows but don’t lose them.”
Advance to reveal: “The join type is a decision, not a default.”







Slide: “block 3: when tools betray you”



Slide: “three approaches to missing earnings”
Show the table. “Same question, three approaches. Drop rows, fill with mean, ignore NaN. They all give the same mean.”
Advance to reveal: “If they all give the same mean, why does it matter?”
“Approach 1 removes entire rows — those schools vanish from every column. Approach 2 shrinks the standard deviation — every imputed value sits at the center. Approach 3 lets pandas skip NaN column by column.”



Slide: bar chart — missing by ownership
“36% of for-profit schools have missing earnings. 9% of public schools. Dropping rows changes WHICH schools remain.”
Advance: “This is informative missingness — the absence of data tells you something about the institution.”



Slide: discussion — “an AI calls .dropna()…”
“An AI ‘cleans’ the Scorecard by calling dropna and reports average earnings of $45,000. Name two questions you’d ask, focused on what was lost in the cleaning step.”
If stuck: “How many rows survived? Are the dropped rows systematically different? What was the missingness rate?”
Key insight: The questions to ask: (1) How many rows survived — what fraction of the data was lost? (2) Are the dropped schools systematically different — were small/for-profit schools disproportionately dropped? (3) Which approach was used and why?



Slide: “Stanford: a case study in hidden complexity”
“Median earnings: $86,000. SAT average: 1465. Sounds good?”
Advance to reveal: “$86K qualifies as ‘Low Income’ in Santa Clara County. The threshold for one person is $111,700.”
Advance to the three factors: “Title IV only — many Stanford students don’t take federal aid. 10 years after enrollment — includes years in grad school. All fields pooled — CS at $139K, English at $31K.”
“The gap between the Scorecard number and your expectations is itself a data-cleaning lesson.”



Slide: “type coercion: tools that corrupt data”
“pd.to_numeric with errors=‘coerce’ converts PrivacySuppressed to NaN. No warning, no count.” Show the defense code. “Always check what you lost.”



Slide: “sentinel values”
Quick. “-999 looks like a number. Mean WITH sentinels: -208°F. Mean WITHOUT: 72.9°F. Unlike NaN, sentinel values participate in every computation.”



Slide: “the identifier trap”
Quick. “OPEID with leading zeros — stored as integer, zeros vanish. Later join against zero-padded strings: zero matches, no error. ZIP codes, phone numbers, IDs: codes, not numbers.”



Slide: discussion — “you upload the Scorecard to ChatGPT…”
“ChatGPT ranks schools by raw MD_EARN_WNE_P10 after dropping all missing data. Stanford is #47. Small groups: identify at least two problems.”
If stuck: “How many rows survived the dropna? What does MD_EARN_WNE_P10 actually measure — and who’s in the sample?”
Key insight: (1) Raw earnings confounded by selectivity — high-SAT schools admit students who’d earn well anywhere. (2) Dropping rows loses most small/for-profit schools. (3) The metric pools all fields and measures 10yr after enrollment. (4) May not be reproducible across sessions.



Slide: “why we write code”
Quick. Two columns: spreadsheets hide logic, code makes decisions visible. “Good code is deterministic.”



Slide: comparison table
Walk through three rows. “Use AI to write code, not to replace understanding.”







Slide: “data munging checklist”
Walk through all 8 items briefly. “This is the checklist you run every time you encounter a new dataset. Each item connects to a concrete example from today.”



Slide: blockquote
Read aloud. Let it sit for 3 seconds.



Slide: “synthesis”
Map each bullet back to the lecture: “Missing data — block 1. Joins — block 2. Every cleaning choice — the whole lecture. Code makes decisions visible — the Reinhart-Rogoff opening. Domain knowledge — the Stanford case study.”



Slide: “next time: linear algebra”
“We can clean and join data. Now we need a language for modeling it. Vectors, linear combinations, column space.”



Slide: “before next class”
“Read Chapter 3. Try the notebook. Quiz 1 is Wednesday — covers Lectures 1 through 3.”



Slide: feedback QR code
“One minute. What was most useful? What was most confusing? Scan the QR code.”






If running long: Skip the identifier trap slide (36 sec) and abbreviate the AI comparison table (just say the punchline).
If running short: Expand the Stanford case study with live notebook exploration of program-level earnings. Show the PrivacySuppressed entries for MS&E. Let students browse their own majors.
Block 3 is the densest section. If time is tight, the checklist slide and AI-vs-code slide can be abbreviated since they recap ideas already demonstrated.
